Back:
Packet switching is the router's core job — the step-by-step process of moving an IP packet from an ingress interface to an egress interface. It happens hundreds of thousands of times per second on modern routers.

Memory: Packet switching = FedEx re-sorting a package at every hub. The BOX (IP packet) is never opened — the SHIPPING LABEL (IP src/dst) stays the same. But the OUTER TRUCK LABEL (L2 MAC) gets re-printed at every hub because each truck only goes so far. TTL = a stamp that says 'expires in N hops' — each hub marks it, zero hops = return to sender.

1.1.a.1 · weight: high

The routing table (also called RIB — Routing Information Base) is the router's database of all known destination networks and how to reach them. Every forwarding decision starts with a routing table lookup.

Memory: Routing table = the router's GPS. Each entry is a memorized destination + route + trust score. CSORD-B mnemonic: 'Cisco Students Often Ride Dirty Buses' — Connected, Static, OSPF, RIP, EIGRP (D), BGP. The brackets [AD/metric] = [how much I trust the source / how expensive the path is].

1.1.a.2 · weight: high

Administrative Distance (AD) is a numeric value from 0-255 that ranks how trustworthy a routing source is. When multiple routing sources advertise the same prefix with the same prefix length, AD is the tiebreaker — the lower AD wins and that route is installed in the routing table.

Memory: AD = trust score, lower = more trusted. 'I saw it with my own eyes' (Connected 0) > 'my boss told me' (Static 1) > 'OSPF told me' (110) > 'RIP whispered it' (120) > 'I don't believe you' (255). Chant it: '0, 1, 20, 90, 110, 115, 120, 170, 200.' Write this list on your exam whiteboard FIRST.

1.1.a.3 · weight: high

Longest prefix match (LPM) is the single most important rule in IP routing. When multiple routes in the routing table match a packet's destination, the router picks the route with the longest subnet mask (the most specific match). This rule runs BEFORE administrative distance and metric are ever considered.

Memory: LPM = most-specific-address-wins, ALWAYS, regardless of source. Like mail: 'USA' matches, 'Illinois' is better, '123 Main St Apt 4B, Chicago IL 60601' wins because it's most specific. /32 > /28 > /24 > /16 > /8 > /0. The mnemonic 'PAM' = Prefix first, AD second, Metric third. NEVER skip to AD.

1.1.a.4 · weight: high

A connected route is automatically installed in the routing table whenever the router has a configured IP address on an interface AND that interface is in up/up state. It's marked with C in show ip route output. You don't need a routing protocol or a static route command — the router infers it from the interface configuration.

Memory: Connected route = 'I can see the neighborhood from my window.' The moment you configure an IP and bring the interface up, the router remembers: 'traffic for this subnet goes out this port.' AD 0 because it's undeniable. Always paired with an L (/32) route for the router's own IP.

1.1.a.5 · weight: high

A static route is manually configured by an administrator to tell the router exactly how to reach a specific network. It's the simplest form of 'telling the router where to send traffic' — no routing protocol overhead, no neighbor relationships, just a direct command.

Memory: Static route = GPS waypoint you hardcode. 'To reach that destination, go through this next hop.' Simple, predictable, but dumb — it doesn't adapt to outages unless you've configured a floating backup. The '1' at the end of 'ip route ... 1' is NOT a typo — you can set custom AD for floating static routes.

1.1.a.6 · weight: high

A dynamic route is one learned automatically by a routing protocol — OSPF, EIGRP, RIP, BGP. Instead of an admin typing each route, routers exchange route information with each other and update their tables as the topology changes.

Memory: Dynamic routing = routers gossiping with each other constantly. 'Hey, I can reach 10.1.1.0/24 with cost 20 via me' → 'Thanks, I'll remember that.' Protocols compete: OSPF = methodical engineer (link-state, calculates shortest path algebraically), EIGRP = fast Cisco kid (hybrid, composite metric), RIP = simple grandpa (hop count, max 15), BGP = diplomat running the internet.

1.1.a.7 · weight: high

Routers do not forward broadcast frames between interfaces by default. This single property is what makes routers fundamentally different from switches — it's why we use routers to divide networks into smaller, manageable pieces.

Memory: Routers = broadcast firewalls by default. Each router interface is the edge of a broadcast domain — broadcasts stop at the router's door and are dropped. Switches spread broadcasts within a VLAN; routers contain them. Rule of thumb: keep broadcast domains under ~250 hosts to prevent broadcast storms from wrecking performance.

1.1.a.8 · weight: high

Cisco Express Forwarding (CEF) is a hardware-accelerated forwarding mechanism used on all modern Cisco routers and L3 switches. It's the 'how' behind fast packet switching — without CEF, every packet would hit the CPU, limiting throughput to tens of thousands of packets per second instead of millions.

Memory: CEF = 'Cisco's Fast lookup.' Two tables: FIB (derived from the routing table) and adjacency (derived from ARP). Pre-built, always ready. Packet arrives → FIB lookup → adjacency lookup → rewrite → out. The CPU never sees the packet for normal traffic. Compare: process switching = CPU per packet (slow); CEF = hardware per packet (fast).

1.1.a.9 · weight: med

Switch learns source MAC → ingress port mappings. Ages out after 300s default on Cisco. View with 'show mac address-table'.

1.1.b.1 · weight: high

If destination MAC is not in the table, switch floods the frame out all ports in the VLAN except the source.

1.1.b.2 · weight: high

Each switch port = separate collision domain. All ports in same VLAN = one broadcast domain. VLANs split broadcast domains.

1.1.b.3 · weight: high

Virtual L3 interface tied to a VLAN on a multilayer switch. 'interface vlan 10' + IP = default gateway for VLAN 10.

1.1.b.4 · weight: high

Physical port configured with 'no switchport' — behaves like a router interface with its own IP.

1.1.b.5 · weight: high

Global command required to enable L3 routing on a multilayer switch. Without it, L3 switch acts only as L2.

1.1.b.6 · weight: high

L3 switches route in hardware (ASICs) at wire speed, faster than CPU-based software routing on most routers.

1.1.b.7 · weight: med

L2 at access layer (endpoint connection). L3 at distribution layer (inter-VLAN routing, policy).

1.1.b.8 · weight: med

Filters packets individually using ACLs — source/dest IP, ports, protocol. No connection awareness.

1.1.c.1 · weight: high

Tracks active connection state (TCP handshake, UDP pseudo-sessions). Auto-allows return traffic. Classic example: Cisco ASA.

1.1.c.2 · weight: high

Adds L7 inspection, application awareness, integrated IPS, URL filtering, AMP, and user identity to stateful firewall capabilities.

1.1.c.3 · weight: high

Inspects L7 payload, identifies specific applications regardless of port (e.g., distinguishes Zoom vs BitTorrent on port 443).

1.1.c.4 · weight: high

Passive monitor on a SPAN/mirror port. Sees COPIES of traffic. Alerts on threats but CANNOT block.

1.1.c.5 · weight: high

Inline device in traffic path. Can DROP malicious packets in real time. Adds latency but actively protects.

1.1.c.6 · weight: high

Matches known attack patterns from an updated database. Reliable for known threats, misses zero-days.

1.1.c.7 · weight: med

Flags traffic deviating from a learned baseline. Can catch novel attacks but produces more false positives.

1.1.c.8 · weight: med

Current Cisco NGFW platform (formerly Firepower Threat Defense). Managed by FMC or Cisco Defense Orchestrator.

1.1.c.9 · weight: low

Standalone AP. Configured individually via CLI/web GUI. All wireless functions local. Fine for 1-3 APs, unmanageable at scale.

1.1.d.1 · weight: high

WLC-managed via CAPWAP. Split-MAC architecture. Scales to thousands of APs with central config.

1.1.d.2 · weight: high

Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points (RFC 5415). Tunnels AP↔WLC traffic. Control = UDP 5246 (DTLS), Data = UDP 5247 (optional DTLS).

1.1.d.3 · weight: high

Real-time RF functions (beacons, encryption, ACKs) stay on AP. Management (auth, SSID/VLAN, RF decisions, firmware) handled by WLC.

1.1.d.4 · weight: high

Default mode. Serves clients and scans for rogues between data frames. Tunnels data to WLC.

1.1.d.5 · weight: med

AP switches data LOCALLY at the branch. Survives WLC failure. Used for remote/branch offices.

1.1.d.6 · weight: high

Monitor = dedicated rogue/IDS sensor, no client service. Sniffer = capture frames for analysis. SE-Connect = spectrum analysis.

1.1.d.7 · weight: low

Basic Service Set = one AP + its clients. BSSID = the AP radio's MAC address.

1.1.d.8 · weight: high

Extended Service Set = multiple APs sharing the same SSID to provide seamless roaming across a larger area.

1.1.d.9 · weight: high

Service Set Identifier. Human-readable network name broadcast in beacon frames.

1.1.d.10 · weight: high

Primary in-band mgmt: AP association, RADIUS auth, admin access. The most critical WLC interface.

1.1.e.1 · weight: high

Default IP 1.1.1.1. Used for DHCP relay, web auth redirect, and mobility manager.

1.1.e.2 · weight: med

Mapped to a VLAN, separates wireless traffic like an SVI on a switch.

1.1.e.3 · weight: med

Out-of-band management only. Not used for client traffic.

1.1.e.4 · weight: low

WLC feature that auto-assigns channels and adjusts transmit power to minimize interference across APs.

1.1.e.5 · weight: med

WLC identifies unauthorized APs on the network via AP scanning.

1.1.e.6 · weight: med

Network-wide management platform. Centerpiece of Intent-Based Networking (IBN). Manages wired + wireless + WAN.

1.1.e.7 · weight: high

Design (hierarchy, IPAM), Policy (SGTs, microseg), Provision (PnP, config push), Assurance (monitoring, AI anomaly detection).

1.1.e.8 · weight: high

DNA Center's network fabric. VXLAN overlay tunnels + LISP for endpoint mobility.

1.1.e.9 · weight: med

WLC = wireless-only controller (APs/WLANs). DNA Center = network-wide controller that can manage WLCs as part of a larger fabric.

1.1.e.10 · weight: high

Device at the edge of a network that generates or consumes data (PC, phone, printer, IP camera, IoT sensor).

1.1.f.1 · weight: high

Network Interface Card. MAC = 48-bit burned-in address (BIA). Unique per NIC.

1.1.f.2 · weight: high

The router or L3 switch SVI an endpoint sends traffic to for destinations outside its local subnet.

1.1.f.3 · weight: high

Lowest campus network tier. Where endpoints connect. Enforces port-level policy.

1.1.f.4 · weight: high

Access-layer feature that limits which MAC addresses can connect on a given port.

1.1.f.5 · weight: high

Port-based auth that requires credentials before granting network access. Works with RADIUS/ISE.

1.1.f.6 · weight: high

Blocks rogue DHCP servers by allowing DHCP offers only from trusted ports.

1.1.f.7 · weight: high

Prevents ARP spoofing by validating ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table.

1.1.f.8 · weight: high

Verify device compliance (patches, AV, cert) before allowing network access.

1.1.f.9 · weight: med

Identity Services Engine. Centralized AAA + 802.1X policy + endpoint profiling.

1.1.f.10 · weight: med

Client initiates a request; server responds. Server listens on a well-known port for incoming connections.

1.1.g.1 · weight: high

Resolves hostnames → IPs. UDP/TCP port 53. Record types: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME, MX, PTR.

1.1.g.2 · weight: high

Assigns IP, mask, GW, DNS. Server UDP 67 / client UDP 68. DORA = Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge.

1.1.g.3 · weight: high

HTTP = TCP 80 (plaintext). HTTPS = TCP 443 (TLS-encrypted).

1.1.g.4 · weight: high

FTP = TCP 21 control + TCP 20 data (active). TFTP = UDP 69, no auth — used for IOS/config transfer.

1.1.g.5 · weight: high

SSH = TCP 22, encrypted. Telnet = TCP 23, plaintext (passwords exposed). Always prefer SSH.

1.1.g.6 · weight: high

SMTP (send) = TCP 25 (or 587). POP3 (retrieve) = TCP 110. IMAP (access) = TCP 143.

1.1.g.7 · weight: high

UDP 1812 auth, UDP 1813 accounting. Open standard. Encrypts password only. Used with 802.1X.

1.1.g.8 · weight: high

TCP 49. Cisco-proprietary AAA. Separates auth/author/acct. Encrypts ENTIRE packet body.

1.1.g.9 · weight: high

UDP 514. Central logging. Severity 0 (emergency) → 7 (debug). Lower = more critical.

1.1.g.10 · weight: high

UDP 123. Time sync across devices. Essential for log correlation and cert validation.

1.1.g.11 · weight: high

UDP 161 queries / UDP 162 traps. v1/v2c = plaintext community strings. v3 = auth + encryption (use v3).

1.1.g.12 · weight: high

The device providing power — typically a PoE switch or a midspan injector.

1.1.h.1 · weight: high

The device drawing power — AP, IP phone, IP camera, etc.

1.1.h.2 · weight: high

Up to 15.4W at PSE (~12.95W at PD). Uses 2 of 4 pairs. Basic IP phones, entry APs.

1.1.h.3 · weight: high

Up to 30W at PSE (~25.5W at PD). 600mA. Modern APs, pan-tilt cameras.

1.1.h.4 · weight: high

Up to 60W using all 4 pairs. Wi-Fi 6 APs, digital signage.

1.1.h.5 · weight: high

Up to 90W using all 4 pairs at higher current. Cat6A recommended. PTZ cams with heaters, laptops.

1.1.h.6 · weight: high

Cisco-proprietary 60W / 90W. Predate and overlap 802.3bt.

1.1.h.7 · weight: low

Switch sends low-voltage probe, checks for 25kΩ signature, then classifies PD into a power class (0-8) to allocate wattage.

1.1.h.8 · weight: med

Total wattage a switch can deliver across all PoE ports. Exceeding it denies power to lower-priority ports.

1.1.h.9 · weight: high

Endspan = PoE built into the switch (common). Midspan = separate injector between non-PoE switch and PD.

1.1.h.10 · weight: med

View PoE status per port — allocated vs consumed watts, device class, port state.

1.1.h.11 · weight: high

Access + combined core/distribution layer. Used in small/medium campuses (~<200 users, single building). Cheaper, simpler, less scalable.

1.2.a.1 · weight: high

Single layer performing inter-VLAN routing, ACLs, QoS, HSRP/VRRP, and high-speed backbone forwarding. Typically L3 (Cat 9300/9500).

1.2.a.2 · weight: high

L2 switches connecting endpoints. Same role as in three-tier: port security, 802.1X, DHCP snooping.

1.2.a.3 · weight: med

First Hop Redundancy Protocols. Allow two switches to share a virtual IP as default gateway. Deployed at collapsed core or distribution.

1.2.a.4 · weight: high

Single building or small multi-building site, <200 users, limited growth. Start here, migrate to three-tier on scale.

1.2.a.5 · weight: med

L2 edge switches connecting endpoints. Port-level VLAN assignment, PoE, 802.1X, port security, DHCP snooping, DAI, QoS trust boundary.

1.2.b.1 · weight: high

L3 aggregation + policy enforcement. Inter-VLAN routing (SVIs), ACLs, QoS enforcement, route summarization, HSRP/VRRP, STP root.

1.2.b.2 · weight: high

High-speed backbone. Pure fast forwarding — NEVER do ACLs, QoS remarking, or policy here. 10G/40G/100G uplinks.

1.2.b.3 · weight: high

Access → Distribution → Core. Top-down or bottom-up, know each layer's function and what it must NOT do.

1.2.b.4 · weight: med

Distribution advertises a single summary route to the core instead of many specific routes. Reduces core routing table size.

1.2.b.5 · weight: high

Each access switch connects to BOTH distribution switches for redundancy. Standard three-tier practice.

1.2.b.6 · weight: high

Scales by adding distribution blocks without redesigning the core. Right for multi-building campuses with thousands of users.

1.2.b.7 · weight: med

Two-tier DC design. Every leaf connects to every spine; leaves never connect to leaves, spines never to spines.

1.2.c.1 · weight: high

Top of Rack. Access layer for servers and storage. Do not cross-connect to other leaves.

1.2.c.2 · weight: high

Backbone. Only connects to leaves — never to servers or other spines. Provides uniform forwarding capacity.

1.2.c.3 · weight: high

Server-to-server within the DC. Dominates virtualized and distributed workloads. Spine-leaf optimizes for this.

1.2.c.4 · weight: high

Client-to-server (in/out of the DC). Three-tier is built for this.

1.2.c.5 · weight: med

L3 routing load-balances across all leaf-spine paths simultaneously. Replaces STP's link-blocking.

1.2.c.6 · weight: high

Any two servers on different leaves are always exactly 2 hops apart (leaf → spine → leaf).

1.2.c.7 · weight: high

Virtual Extensible LAN. Extends L2 segments across L3 spine-leaf fabric. Paired with EVPN (BGP-based control plane).

1.2.c.8 · weight: med

Wide Area Network. Connects geographically separated sites over provider infrastructure. Slower, higher-latency, more expensive than LAN.

1.2.d.1 · weight: high

Multiprotocol Label Switching. Provider-managed private WAN with guaranteed SLAs. L3 VPN (provider routes) or L2 VPN. Expensive, reliable.

1.2.d.2 · weight: high

Ethernet-based WAN within a metro area. Types: E-Line (P2P), E-LAN (any-to-any), E-Tree (hub-and-spoke).

1.2.d.3 · weight: med

Consumer/business internet access. Cheap, no SLA guarantees. Common as SD-WAN underlay or backup.

1.2.d.4 · weight: med

Dedicated P2P circuit with guaranteed bandwidth. T1=1.544 Mbps, T3=44.736 Mbps. Legacy, being replaced by Metro Ethernet/MPLS.

1.2.d.5 · weight: med

Software-Defined WAN. Encrypted overlay across multiple transports (MPLS, broadband, LTE). Central controller, app-aware path selection.

1.2.d.6 · weight: high

Customer Premises Equipment. The router at the customer site, typically the demarc boundary for managed services.

1.2.d.7 · weight: med

Boundary between provider-managed and customer-managed infrastructure. Provider owns up to the demarc.

1.2.d.8 · weight: med

MPLS = guaranteed SLA, expensive, provider-routed. SD-WAN = app-aware, cheaper, uses multiple transports, centrally managed. Strategic direction: SD-WAN.

1.2.d.9 · weight: high

Small Office/Home Office. 1–10 users. Simple flat topology with a single all-in-one device.

1.2.e.1 · weight: high

Combines router, switch (4-8 ports), AP, firewall, DHCP server, and NAT gateway in one box.

1.2.e.2 · weight: high

Single subnet and single broadcast domain. No VLANs, no inter-VLAN routing, no core/distribution layers.

1.2.e.3 · weight: med

Many private IPs → single public IP using port numbers to distinguish sessions. The common form of NAT at a SOHO.

1.2.e.4 · weight: high

SOHO: one device, no redundancy, no advanced features. Enterprise: tiered, redundant, centrally managed.

1.2.e.6 · weight: med

Organization owns, operates, hosts all infrastructure. Full control, full responsibility. CapEx-heavy, slow to scale.

1.2.f.1 · weight: high

Third-party-hosted infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GCP). Rented on demand. OpEx model, elastic scaling, shared responsibility.

1.2.f.2 · weight: high

CapEx = Capital Expenditure (upfront hardware purchase, on-prem). OpEx = Operational Expenditure (pay-as-you-go, cloud).

1.2.f.3 · weight: high

Infrastructure as a Service. Provider manages HW + virtualization. You manage OS, apps, data. Example: AWS EC2.

1.2.f.4 · weight: high

Platform as a Service. Provider manages HW + OS + runtime. You manage app + data. Example: AWS Elastic Beanstalk.

1.2.f.5 · weight: high

Software as a Service. Provider manages everything. You just use the app. Example: M365, Salesforce.

1.2.f.6 · weight: high

Provider secures infrastructure; customer secures data, access, app config. Split varies by service model.

1.2.f.7 · weight: high

Mix of on-prem + cloud. Sensitive workloads on-prem, elastic workloads in cloud.

1.2.f.8 · weight: med

Dedicated links to cloud providers. AWS Direct Connect, Azure ExpressRoute, Google Cloud Interconnect. Bypass public internet.

1.2.f.9 · weight: med

8-10μm core. Laser transmitters. 100+ km. Yellow jacket. Used for long-haul WAN and campus building-to-building backbone.

1.3.a.1 · weight: high

50 or 62.5μm core. LED/VCSEL transmitters. Up to ~550m at 10G (OM3/OM4). Orange (OM1/OM2) or aqua (OM3/OM4) jacket.

1.3.a.2 · weight: high

Multiple light modes in MMF arrive at slightly different times → distance limit. Why MMF has shorter reach than SMF.

1.3.a.3 · weight: med

LC (Lucent, small form-factor, most common) and SC (Subscriber, square push-pull). Both used on SMF and MMF.

1.3.a.4 · weight: med

UTP copper. 1 Gbps at 100m max. Minimum standard for modern networks.

1.3.a.5 · weight: high

UTP copper. 1 Gbps at 100m OR 10 Gbps at 55m max. Better shielding than Cat5e.

1.3.a.6 · weight: high

UTP copper. 10 Gbps at full 100m. Required for 10GBASE-T at max distance.

1.3.a.7 · weight: high

Unshielded twisted pair copper tops out at 100 meters (328 ft) regardless of category.

1.3.a.8 · weight: high

Hot-swappable transceivers. SFP=1G, SFP+=10G, QSFP+=40G, QSFP28=100G. Determines port's cable type.

1.3.a.9 · weight: high

Legacy hub-based or coax 10BASE2/5. All devices share one collision domain. Half-duplex only.

1.3.b.1 · weight: high

L1 device that repeats signals to all ports. No MAC awareness. One collision domain total. Obsolete.

1.3.b.2 · weight: high

Modern switched design. Each device has a dedicated link to its switch port. No collisions, full-duplex.

1.3.b.3 · weight: high

Segment where simultaneous transmissions collide. Hub = 1 total. Switch = 1 PER PORT (micro-segmentation).

1.3.b.4 · weight: high

Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection. Listen before transmit, detect collisions, random backoff. Needed only in half-duplex shared media.

1.3.b.5 · weight: high

Half-duplex = one direction at a time (hub/shared). Full-duplex = both directions simultaneously (switched point-to-point).

1.3.b.6 · weight: high

Each switch port = its own collision domain. Key benefit of switched networks over hubs.

1.3.b.7 · weight: high

Two devices transmit on a shared collision domain at the same time; frames corrupt. Normal on half-duplex, never on full-duplex.

1.4.a.1 · weight: high

Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection. Listen before transmit, detect collision, jam, random backoff, retry.

1.4.a.2 · weight: high

Collision window — collisions detected within the first 64 bytes are normal on half-duplex. Anything after 64 bytes is a late collision.

1.4.a.3 · weight: high

Collision detected after 64 bytes. Always abnormal. #1 symptom of duplex mismatch.

1.4.a.4 · weight: high

Duplex mismatch (most common), cable > 100m, faulty NIC.

1.4.a.5 · weight: high

Should be zero. Nonzero = something broken (duplex mismatch or hardware).

1.4.a.6 · weight: high

Frame Check Sequence. 32-bit checksum appended to each Ethernet frame. Receiver recalculates and compares.

1.4.b.1 · weight: high

FCS mismatch at receiver → frame discarded. Counter increments. Always a Layer 1 problem.

1.4.b.2 · weight: high

Bad/damaged cable (#1), EMI, faulty NIC or port, duplex mismatch, dirty fiber connectors.

1.4.b.3 · weight: high

input errors = CRC + runts + giants + frame + overrun + ignored. Identify which sub-counter is rising.

1.4.b.4 · weight: high

Replace cable → check EMI → different port → replace NIC → check duplex. Always bottom-up.

1.4.b.5 · weight: med

64 bytes. Anything smaller is a runt. Includes 14-byte header + 46-byte min payload + 4-byte FCS.

1.4.c.1 · weight: high

1518 bytes standard. 1522 bytes with a single 802.1Q tag. Anything larger is a giant.

1.4.c.2 · weight: high

Frame < 64 bytes. Collision fragment on half-duplex (normal). On full-duplex = duplex mismatch or bad NIC.

1.4.c.3 · weight: high

Frame > 1518 bytes (or > 1522 on trunk). Caused by MTU mismatch or faulty NIC.

1.4.c.4 · weight: high

Slightly over 1518 due to 802.1Q (1522), Q-in-Q (1526), or MPLS labels. Some switches accept via 'system mtu' config.

1.4.c.5 · weight: med

One side configured for jumbo (9000 bytes), other for standard 1518 → giants on the receiving side.

1.4.c.6 · weight: med

Total count of all inbound frame problems (CRC + runts + giants + frame + overrun + ignored).

1.4.d.1 · weight: high

Couldn't transmit a frame. Usually congestion (TX queue full). Often paired with output drops.

1.4.d.2 · weight: high

Frames discarded from the output queue because it was full. Symptom of congestion.

1.4.d.3 · weight: med

Interface reinitialized. Causes: shut/no-shut, keepalive failure, cable unplug, link flap.

1.4.d.4 · weight: med

Count of down→up transitions. High = link flapping, usually a physical-layer problem.

1.4.d.5 · weight: high

Input buffer overflowed or frames ignored because buffer was full. Switch is overwhelmed.

1.4.d.6 · weight: med

Reset interface counters to zero. Use to measure error rates within a specific window.

1.4.d.7 · weight: med

One side full-duplex, the other half-duplex. Link shows up/up but performance is terrible.

1.4.e.1 · weight: high

Late collisions + FCS errors. Full-duplex side transmits whenever it wants; half-duplex detects it as collisions.

1.4.e.2 · weight: high

CRC/FCS errors + runts. Collision-aborted frames from half-duplex side arrive as corrupted fragments.

1.4.e.3 · weight: high

One side manual, other auto. Auto can't negotiate — falls back to IEEE defaults → mismatch.

1.4.e.4 · weight: high

Both sides auto (preferred) OR both sides manually set to identical speed+duplex.

1.4.e.5 · weight: high

show interfaces status (fast scan), show interfaces [int] (error counters), show cdp neighbors detail (remote info).

1.4.e.6 · weight: med

Different speeds on each side. Link usually fails to come up at all (down/down). Easier to spot than duplex mismatch.

1.4.f.1 · weight: high

Two sides exchange capabilities and pick best common speed/duplex. Requires BOTH sides set to auto.

1.4.f.2 · weight: high

When negotiation fails, auto side senses speed from electrical signal (usually correct).

1.4.f.3 · weight: med

When negotiation fails: half-duplex for 10/100 Mbps, full-duplex for 1000 Mbps. Source of duplex mismatch.

1.4.f.4 · weight: high

Both sides auto-negotiate. If manual, match both sides exactly. Never one manual + one auto.

1.4.f.5 · weight: high

Both L1 and L2 operational. Interface working normally. Desired state.

1.4.g.1 · weight: high

L1 up but L2 protocol failed. Causes: encapsulation mismatch, keepalive failure, missing clock rate on DCE serial.

1.4.g.2 · weight: high

L1 failure. Check cable, connectors, remote power, remote interface, SFP/transceiver.

1.4.g.3 · weight: high

Interface manually disabled with 'shutdown'. Bring up with 'no shutdown'.

1.4.g.4 · weight: high

Shutdown by default. You must 'no shutdown' after configuring.

1.4.g.5 · weight: high

NOT shutdown by default. Comes up automatically when a cable is connected.

1.4.g.6 · weight: high

Primary command for status code check. Shows interface, IP, Status, Protocol columns.

1.4.g.7 · weight: high

Deep per-interface view. MAC, MTU, bandwidth, duplex, speed, and all error counters.

1.4.h.1 · weight: high

Summary table of all ports: Port, Name, Status, Vlan, Duplex, Speed, Type. Fastest way to scan for problems.

1.4.h.2 · weight: high

connected (up/up), notconnect (down/down), disabled (shutdown), err-disabled (auto-shut).

1.4.h.3 · weight: high

Switch auto-shutdown due to security violation (port security), BPDU guard, or error condition. Requires shut/no-shut or errdisable recovery to restore.

1.4.h.4 · weight: high

L3-focused: interface name, IP, OK?, Method, Status, Protocol. Use for IP and up/down check.

1.4.h.5 · weight: high

Trunk-specific: native VLAN, allowed VLANs, encapsulation, trunking mode.

1.4.h.6 · weight: high

Means auto-negotiated result (e.g., a-full = auto-negotiated to full-duplex).

1.4.h.7 · weight: med

Transmission Control Protocol. L4, connection-oriented, reliable, ordered delivery.

1.5.a.1 · weight: high

Every byte of data has a sequence number. Enables ordered reassembly and duplicate detection.

1.5.a.2 · weight: high

Receiver confirms received bytes. Missing ACK triggers retransmission at sender.

1.5.a.3 · weight: high

Sender resends unacknowledged data after timeout. Core reliability mechanism.

1.5.a.4 · weight: high

Receiver advertises window size — how much unACK'd data sender may transmit.

1.5.a.5 · weight: high

Slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit/recovery. Adjusts send rate to avoid loss.

1.5.a.6 · weight: med

20 bytes minimum, 60 bytes maximum (with options). Larger than UDP.

1.5.a.7 · weight: high

SYN (synchronize), ACK (acknowledge), FIN (finish), RST (reset), PSH (push), URG (urgent).

1.5.a.8 · weight: high

SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. Establishes TCP connection and synchronizes sequence numbers.

1.5.b.1 · weight: high

Client sends SYN flag with its Initial Sequence Number (ISN). Enters SYN-SENT state.

1.5.b.2 · weight: high

Server responds with SYN + ACK. Carries server's ISN and ACKs client's SYN (ack = client ISN + 1).

1.5.b.3 · weight: high

Client ACKs server's SYN (ack = server ISN + 1). Both sides enter ESTABLISHED.

1.5.b.4 · weight: high

Random 32-bit starting sequence number each side chooses. Exchanged during handshake.

1.5.b.5 · weight: med

Largest TCP payload each side will accept. Negotiated in the handshake options.

1.5.b.6 · weight: med

Both sides completed handshake; ready for application data transfer.

1.5.b.7 · weight: high

FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK. Each direction of the full-duplex connection closes independently.

1.5.c.1 · weight: high

'I have no more data to send.' Initiates graceful close of one direction.

1.5.c.2 · weight: high

After one side FINs, it can still receive. That direction closes, other stays open until its own FIN.

1.5.c.3 · weight: med

After final ACK, client waits 2× MSL (max segment lifetime, ~60-120s) to catch delayed segments.

1.5.c.4 · weight: med

Abrupt connection termination. No FIN/ACK exchange. Used for refused connections, crashes, or firewall denies.

1.5.c.5 · weight: high

FIN = graceful (hang-up after goodbye). RST = abrupt (slam phone down).

1.5.c.6 · weight: high

Bytes the sender can transmit before waiting for an ACK. Dynamically advertised by receiver.

1.5.d.1 · weight: high

Receiver buffer full. Tells sender to stop transmitting until an update.

1.5.d.2 · weight: high

Connection begins with small window; doubles on each RTT (exponential) until ssthresh.

1.5.d.3 · weight: med

After ssthresh, window grows linearly (additive increase) to avoid overshooting capacity.

1.5.d.4 · weight: med

3 duplicate ACKs → retransmit missing segment immediately without waiting for timeout.

1.5.d.5 · weight: med

Ordering (reassembly), duplicate detection, and ACK reporting ('send me byte N next').

1.5.d.6 · weight: high

User Datagram Protocol. L4, connectionless, best-effort, no reliability, low overhead.

1.5.e.1 · weight: high

8 bytes only. Four fields: source port, destination port, length, checksum.

1.5.e.2 · weight: high

No handshake, no connection state. Sender just sends datagrams.

1.5.e.3 · weight: high

No ACKs, no retransmission. Lost datagrams stay lost unless app implements its own reliability.

1.5.e.4 · weight: high

Datagrams can arrive out of order; no sequence numbers to reassemble.

1.5.e.5 · weight: high

VoIP, video streaming/conferencing, online gaming, DNS queries, DHCP, SNMP, Syslog, NTP, TFTP.

1.5.e.6 · weight: high

FTP data channel (active mode). Carries the actual file bytes.

1.5.f.1 · weight: high

FTP command channel. Maintains the session (login, ls, get, put).

1.5.f.2 · weight: high

Secure Shell. Encrypted remote CLI access. Replaced Telnet. Always use this.

1.5.f.3 · weight: high

Unencrypted remote CLI. Passwords in plaintext. Never use in production.

1.5.f.4 · weight: high

Simple Mail Transfer Protocol. Server-to-server mail relay. Port 587 used for authenticated submission.

1.5.f.5 · weight: high

Unencrypted web. Session cookies and credentials exposed.

1.5.f.6 · weight: high

Post Office Protocol v3. Retrieves email; typically deletes from server.

1.5.f.7 · weight: high

Internet Message Access Protocol. Access email kept on server. Multi-device sync.

1.5.f.8 · weight: high

HTTP over TLS. Encrypted web. Standard for all modern sites.

1.5.f.9 · weight: high

0-1023 well-known (servers). 1024-49151 registered. 49152-65535 ephemeral (client-side).

1.5.f.10 · weight: high

Standard name resolution queries. Small, stateless, fast. >99% of DNS uses UDP.

1.5.g.1 · weight: high

Server listens on 67. Receives Discover/Request from clients.

1.5.g.2 · weight: high

Client listens on 68. Receives Offer/Acknowledge from server.

1.5.g.3 · weight: high

Trivial FTP. No authentication, no directory listing. Used for router/switch config and IOS transfer.

1.5.g.4 · weight: high

Network Time Protocol. Clock sync across devices. Critical for log correlation and cert validation.

1.5.g.5 · weight: high

Management station sends GET/SET to device on 161.

1.5.g.6 · weight: high

Device sends unsolicited alert to management station on 162.

1.5.g.7 · weight: high

Centralized logging destination port. Severity levels 0-7.

1.5.g.8 · weight: high

0 Emergency, 1 Alert, 2 Critical, 3 Error, 4 Warning, 5 Notification, 6 Informational, 7 Debug. Lower = more severe.

1.5.g.9 · weight: high

Default for standard name queries. Small, fast. Used for ~99% of DNS traffic.

1.5.h.1 · weight: high

Used for (1) zone transfers and (2) truncated UDP responses. Same port as UDP.

1.5.h.2 · weight: high

Set in UDP DNS response when it's too big. Tells client to retry over TCP.

1.5.h.3 · weight: high

Full zone transfer from primary DNS server to secondary. Uses TCP 53.

1.5.h.4 · weight: med

Incremental zone transfer — only changes since last transfer. Uses TCP 53.

1.5.h.5 · weight: med

DNS is the canonical example — one port (53) serving both TCP and UDP with different roles.

1.5.h.6 · weight: high

32-bit number written as 4 octets in dotted-decimal (e.g., 192.168.1.10).

1.6.a.1 · weight: high

8 bits, valid range 0-255. Four octets make an IPv4 address.

1.6.a.2 · weight: high

Subnet mask defines boundary. Left bits = network, right bits = host.

1.6.a.3 · weight: high

128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1. Powers of 2 from 2^7 to 2^0. Sum to 255.

1.6.a.4 · weight: high

All host bits set to 0. Identifies the subnet. NOT assignable to a host.

1.6.a.5 · weight: high

All host bits set to 1. Reaches all hosts in the subnet. NOT assignable to a host.

1.6.a.6 · weight: high

IP AND subnet mask = network address. How a router determines which subnet an IP belongs to.

1.6.a.7 · weight: high

2^32 ≈ 4.3 billion addresses total. Exhaustion drove IPv6 creation.

1.6.a.8 · weight: med

First octet 1-126. Default /8. Leading bit 0. ~16.7M hosts/network. Example: 10.0.0.0/8.

1.6.b.1 · weight: high

First octet 128-191. Default /16. Leading bits 10. 65,534 hosts/network. Example: 172.16.0.0/16.

1.6.b.2 · weight: high

First octet 192-223. Default /24. Leading bits 110. 254 hosts/network. Example: 192.168.1.0/24.

1.6.b.3 · weight: high

First octet 224-239. Leading bits 1110. Reserved for multicast. Not assignable to hosts.

1.6.b.4 · weight: high

First octet 240-255. Leading bits 1111. Experimental/reserved. Never publicly assigned.

1.6.b.5 · weight: med

127 is NOT Class A — it's loopback. Exam trick: first-octet ranges skip 127.

1.6.b.6 · weight: high

1993. CIDR = Classless Inter-Domain Routing. Allows any mask length, not just class defaults.

1.6.b.7 · weight: med

32-bit value. Contiguous 1s (network) followed by contiguous 0s (host). 0 after 1 is INVALID.

1.6.c.1 · weight: high

Counts the 1-bits. /24 = 24 ones = 255.255.255.0. Must convert instantly both directions.

1.6.c.2 · weight: high

255.255.255.0. 256 addresses, 254 usable hosts. Class C default.

1.6.c.3 · weight: high

255.255.255.128. 128 addresses, 126 usable hosts. Block size 128.

1.6.c.4 · weight: high

255.255.255.192. 64 addresses, 62 usable hosts. Block size 64.

1.6.c.5 · weight: high

255.255.255.224. 32 addresses, 30 usable hosts. Block size 32.

1.6.c.6 · weight: high

255.255.255.240. 16 addresses, 14 usable hosts. Block size 16.

1.6.c.7 · weight: high

255.255.255.248. 8 addresses, 6 usable hosts. Block size 8.

1.6.c.8 · weight: high

255.255.255.252. 4 addresses, 2 usable. Standard for point-to-point links.

1.6.c.9 · weight: high

255.255.255.254. 2 addresses, 2 usable (RFC 3021). P2P-only.

1.6.c.10 · weight: med

255.255.255.255. Single host route.

1.6.c.11 · weight: med

Block size = 256 − interesting-octet mask value. Subnets start at multiples of block size.

1.6.d.1 · weight: high

The octet where the mask transitions from 1s to 0s. Do your math here.

1.6.d.2 · weight: high

Round IP's interesting octet DOWN to the nearest multiple of the block size. All host bits = 0.

1.6.d.3 · weight: high

Next network − 1. Or: all host bits = 1.

1.6.d.4 · weight: high

Network address + 1.

1.6.d.5 · weight: high

Broadcast address − 1.

1.6.d.6 · weight: high

Number of subnets = 2^(borrowed bits). Each borrowed bit doubles subnets.

1.6.e.1 · weight: high

Hosts per subnet = 2^(host bits) − 2. Minus 2 for network and broadcast.

1.6.e.2 · weight: high

Bits taken from host portion and given to network portion. Mask /26 from /24 default = 2 borrowed.

1.6.e.3 · weight: high

/25=126, /26=62, /27=30, /28=14, /29=6, /30=2. Memorize cold.

1.6.e.4 · weight: high

Need 50 hosts → 2^6 − 2 = 62 (fits). Always round UP to the next fitting power.

1.6.e.5 · weight: high

Variable Length Subnet Mask. Use different mask lengths within one major network to right-size each subnet.

1.6.f.1 · weight: high

Always allocate the largest required subnet first, then next-largest, and so on.

1.6.f.2 · weight: high

Each new subnet starts right after the previous subnet's broadcast address.

1.6.f.3 · weight: high

OSPF, EIGRP, RIPv2 support VLSM. They include subnet mask in routing updates.

1.6.f.4 · weight: high

Classful protocols — CANNOT support VLSM. Assume default classful mask.

1.6.f.5 · weight: med

Exactly 2 usable hosts. Standard for router-to-router serial/point-to-point.

1.6.f.6 · weight: high

128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1. Powers of 2 from 2^7 to 2^0. Sum to 255. The 'magic 8'.

1.6.g.1 · weight: high

Sum the positional values of all 1-bits. 11001010 = 128+64+8+2 = 202.

1.6.g.2 · weight: high

Subtract largest fitting power of 2, mark a 1, repeat. Always write all 8 bits.

1.6.g.3 · weight: high

0, 128, 192, 224, 240, 248, 252, 254, 255. Each adds one more 1-bit from the left.

1.6.g.4 · weight: high

Each hexadecimal character represents exactly 4 bits. 0-9 = 0-9, A-F = 10-15.

1.6.g.5 · weight: high

A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15.

1.6.g.6 · weight: high

Multiply each digit by its positional 16-value. 0xCA = C(12)×16 + A(10)×1 = 202.

1.6.g.7 · weight: med

Compact table of all interfaces: IP, Status (L1), Protocol (L2). First stop for connectivity issues.

1.6.h.1 · weight: high

Full routing table. Route source code, network, AD/metric, next-hop, interface, age.

1.6.h.2 · weight: high

Auto-added when interface comes up/up. AD 0.

1.6.h.3 · weight: high

/32 host route for the router's own interface IP.

1.6.h.4 · weight: high

Manually configured static route. AD 1.

1.6.h.5 · weight: high

O = OSPF, D = EIGRP (Dual-algorithm), R = RIP. Dynamically learned routes.

1.6.h.6 · weight: high

Static default route (0.0.0.0/0) marked as candidate for gateway of last resort.

1.6.h.7 · weight: high

ICMP Echo Request. !!!!! = success, ..... = failure, U = unreachable. L3 reachability test.

1.6.h.8 · weight: high

Shows every hop along the path to destination. Identifies where packets drop.

1.6.h.9 · weight: high

Private Class A. All 10.x.x.x addresses. 16,777,214 hosts. Largest RFC 1918 range.

1.7.a.1 · weight: high

Dominant internal range for large enterprises, data centers, cloud providers. CoreWeave, AWS, Azure.

1.7.a.2 · weight: med

Internet routers drop packets with 10.x.x.x source or destination. Requires NAT to reach internet.

1.7.a.3 · weight: high

Valid range is 172.16.0.0 through 172.31.255.255. NOT 172.0-172.255.

1.7.b.1 · weight: high

First 12 bits fixed. Second octet has 4 free bits = 0000(16) through 1111(31).

1.7.b.2 · weight: med

PUBLIC. Below the 172.16 start of the private range.

1.7.b.3 · weight: high

PUBLIC. Above the 172.31 end of the private range.

1.7.b.4 · weight: high

172.17.0.0/16 — Docker's default bridge network, within the RFC 1918 Class B block.

1.7.b.5 · weight: low

Private Class C. All 192.168.x.x addresses. 65,534 hosts. Familiar from home networks.

1.7.c.1 · weight: high

Almost every consumer router uses 192.168.0.0/24 or 192.168.1.0/24 with gateway .1.

1.7.c.2 · weight: high

192.168.x.x = PRIVATE. 192.169.x.x = PUBLIC. Exact boundary on second octet.

1.7.c.3 · weight: high

Defines the three private IPv4 ranges: 10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16. Not routable on public internet.

1.7.d.1 · weight: high

Network Address Translation. Rewrites IP addresses at a boundary so private addresses can reach the internet.

1.7.d.2 · weight: high

One-to-one permanent mapping. One private IP ↔ one public IP. Used for servers needing consistent external access.

1.7.d.3 · weight: high

Many-to-many from a pool. First-come basis. Exhausts if pool fills.

1.7.d.4 · weight: med

Many-to-one using ports to differentiate. Command keyword 'overload'. What home routers do.

1.7.d.5 · weight: high

Private IP of internal host (e.g., 192.168.1.10). The 'real' internal address.

1.7.d.6 · weight: high

Public IP that internal host is translated to (e.g., 203.0.113.5). What the internet sees.

1.7.d.7 · weight: high

IP of external host as it appears from inside (usually same as Outside Global).

1.7.d.8 · weight: med

Actual public IP of the external host (e.g., 8.8.8.8).

1.7.d.9 · weight: med

View current NAT translation table. show ip nat statistics shows hit counts.

1.7.d.10 · weight: high

Automatic Private IP Addressing. 169.254.0.0/16 link-local range. IPv4 fallback when DHCP fails.

1.7.e.1 · weight: high

If you see 169.254.x.x on a host → DHCP server unreachable, no DHCP response received.

1.7.e.2 · weight: high

169.254.x.x is link-local only. Cannot reach other subnets or the internet.

1.7.e.3 · weight: high

RFC 3927. Cisco devices do NOT use APIPA; unconfigured Cisco interfaces simply have no IP.

1.7.e.4 · weight: med

Check cable, DHCP service, 'ip helper-address' on gateway, then ipconfig /release + /renew.

1.7.e.5 · weight: high

Entire /8 reserved for loopback. Traffic never leaves the local device.

1.7.f.1 · weight: high

Most-used loopback address. Ping tests the local TCP/IP stack.

1.7.f.2 · weight: high

Exam trick: 127.5.5.5 is ALSO loopback. The whole /8 counts, not just .0.0.1.

1.7.f.3 · weight: high

127.0.0.1 (address) is NOT the same as a Cisco loopback interface (virtual, routable, used for router ID/BGP).

1.7.f.4 · weight: high

(1) ping 127.0.0.1 (stack), (2) own IP (NIC), (3) gateway (local link), (4) remote host (full path).

1.7.f.5 · weight: med

128 bits. 2^128 ≈ 340 undecillion addresses. 4× larger than IPv4's 32 bits.

1.8.a.1 · weight: high

One of the 8 groups in an IPv6 address. 16 bits = 4 hex digits. Separated by colons.

1.8.a.2 · weight: high

Fixed 40 bytes. Simpler than IPv4 — no checksum, no fragmentation fields, options in extension headers.

1.8.a.3 · weight: high

IPv6 eliminates broadcast entirely. Replaced by multicast and anycast.

1.8.a.4 · weight: high

Hosts can generate their own addresses via Stateless Address Autoconfiguration. No DHCP required.

1.8.a.5 · weight: high

Standard IPv6 LAN: first 64 bits = network prefix, last 64 bits = interface ID.

1.8.a.6 · weight: high

IPv4 exhaustion (last /8 allocated 2011). NAT added complexity. IPv6 removes need for NAT.

1.8.a.7 · weight: med

Remove leading zeros in each hextet. 0DB8 → DB8, 0001 → 1, 0000 → 0. Must keep at least one digit.

1.8.b.1 · weight: high

Replace ONE consecutive run of all-zero hextets with ::. Use for the longest run.

1.8.b.2 · weight: high

Double colon can only appear once per address. Two would be ambiguous.

1.8.b.3 · weight: high

Count visible hextets; subtract from 8; fill :: with that many 0000 groups.

1.8.b.4 · weight: high

The unspecified address (all zeros). Equivalent to IPv4 0.0.0.0.

1.8.b.5 · weight: med

IPv6 loopback address. Equivalent to IPv4 127.0.0.1.

1.8.b.6 · weight: high

Every individual IPv6 LAN segment. Required for SLAAC (interface ID must be 64 bits).

1.8.c.1 · weight: high

Typical block from ISP to organization. 16 bits available for internal subnetting → 65,536 /64 subnets.

1.8.c.2 · weight: high

Recommended for router-to-router P2P links (RFC 6164). 2 addresses, no waste. IPv6 analogue of IPv4 /30.

1.8.c.3 · weight: high

Single address. Used for loopback interfaces and specific host routes.

1.8.c.4 · weight: high

IPv6 standard LAN is always /64. You don't calculate IPv6 subnets on CCNA the way you do for IPv4.

1.8.c.5 · weight: med

Stateless Address Autoconfiguration. Host self-generates IPv6 address from RA prefix + interface ID. No DHCP required.

1.8.d.1 · weight: high

ICMPv6 Type 133. Host sends to FF02::2 asking for network config.

1.8.d.2 · weight: high

ICMPv6 Type 134. Router responds with prefix, default gateway (its link-local), and A/O/M flags.

1.8.d.3 · weight: high

Generated from MAC by inserting FFFE in middle and flipping 7th bit. 64 bits.

1.8.d.4 · weight: high

RFC 7217. Random interface ID instead of EUI-64 to prevent MAC tracking. Default on modern OSes.

1.8.d.5 · weight: med

Host sends NS for its own address before claiming it. No reply = address is unique.

1.8.d.6 · weight: high

Address Autoconfiguration flag in RA. A=1 tells host to use SLAAC for addressing.

1.8.d.7 · weight: high

Hybrid: SLAAC provides the address, DHCPv6 provides DNS/domain/NTP. Server does NOT track address assignments.

1.8.e.1 · weight: high

Other Configuration. O=1 in RA tells host to query DHCPv6 for DNS/other info (but not address).

1.8.e.2 · weight: high

All DHCPv6 servers/relay agents multicast address. Clients send Information-Request here.

1.8.e.3 · weight: med

Recursive DNS Server option in RA. Modern alternative to stateless DHCPv6 for DNS.

1.8.e.4 · weight: low

'ipv6 nd other-config-flag' under interface sets O=1 in outgoing RAs.

1.8.e.5 · weight: med

DHCPv6 server assigns full address and tracks it in a binding table. Like DHCPv4 for IPv6.

1.8.f.1 · weight: high

Managed Address Configuration. M=1 in RA tells host to get its address from DHCPv6 server.

1.8.f.2 · weight: high

Solicit → Advertise → Request → Reply. Four-step DHCPv6 exchange. Analogous to DORA in DHCPv4.

1.8.f.3 · weight: high

Even with stateful DHCPv6, the default gateway comes from the Router Advertisement, NOT DHCPv6. Classic exam gotcha.

1.8.f.4 · weight: high

FE80:: link-local is always self-generated via SLAAC, regardless of which method is used for the global address.

1.8.f.5 · weight: high

'ipv6 nd managed-config-flag' under interface sets M=1 in outgoing RAs.

1.8.f.6 · weight: med

Neighbor Discovery Protocol. Uses ICMPv6. Replaces ARP + ICMP Router Discovery + ICMP Redirect from IPv4.

1.8.g.1 · weight: high

Router Solicitation. Host → FF02::2 asking for router info.

1.8.g.2 · weight: high

Router Advertisement. Router → FF02::1 with prefix, gateway, flags. Also sent periodically every 200s.

1.8.g.3 · weight: high

Neighbor Solicitation. Replaces ARP Request. Resolves IPv6 → MAC. Also used for DAD.

1.8.g.4 · weight: high

Neighbor Advertisement. Replaces ARP Reply. Carries MAC. Can be unsolicited.

1.8.g.5 · weight: high

FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx. Last 24 bits of unicast. Efficient NS delivery; avoids full broadcast.

1.8.g.6 · weight: high

Neighbor cache view. IPv6 equivalent of 'show arp'.

1.8.g.7 · weight: high

GLOBAL command. Master switch for IPv6 routing. Without it, router won't forward IPv6 or send RAs.

1.8.h.1 · weight: high

Interface command. Assigns global unicast. Auto-enables IPv6 and generates a link-local.

1.8.h.2 · weight: high

Interface command. Enables IPv6 and generates ONLY link-local (no global). For local-only comms.

1.8.h.3 · weight: med

Manually set link-local to a memorable value. Good practice for router identity.

1.8.h.4 · weight: med

IPv6 analogue of show ip interface brief. Interface, addresses, status.

1.8.h.5 · weight: high

IPv6 routing table. Codes: C (connected), L (local /128), S (static), O (OSPFv3), D (EIGRP for IPv6).

1.8.h.6 · weight: high

Test IPv6 reachability. Same behavior as IPv4 ping.

1.8.h.7 · weight: high

Prefix 2000::/3. Starts with 2 or 3. IPv6 equivalent of public IPv4. Internet-routable.

1.9.a.1 · weight: high

FC00::/7, in practice FD00::/8. Starts with FD. IPv6 equivalent of RFC 1918 private. Not internet-routable.

1.9.a.2 · weight: high

FE80::/10. Auto-generated on every IPv6-enabled interface. Never forwarded by routers.

1.9.a.3 · weight: high

Every IPv6 interface MUST have a link-local. Used for NDP, routing protocol hellos, next-hop entries.

1.9.a.4 · weight: high

Global Routing Prefix (from ISP, ~/48) + Subnet ID (~16 bits) + Interface ID (64 bits).

1.9.a.5 · weight: med

2 or 3 → GUA. FD → ULA. FE80 → LLA. FF → multicast. Drill to instant recognition.

1.9.a.6 · weight: high

Same address on multiple devices. Routing delivers to the nearest. One-to-one-of-many.

1.9.b.1 · weight: high

Syntactically identical to unicast. The distinction is deployment, not format.

1.9.b.2 · weight: high

DNS root servers, CDN POPs, load balancing, redundancy. 8.8.8.8 is an anycast in IPv4 too.

1.9.b.3 · weight: med

'ipv6 address [addr]/128 anycast' on interface. Typically /128 so exact address is advertised.

1.9.b.4 · weight: med

Address with all host bits = 0 in a /64. All routers on that subnet auto-respond.

2.5.b.7 · weight: high

Port transitions immediately from discarding to forwarding. ACCESS PORTS to endpoints only.

3.1.d.4 · weight: high

3.1.e.1 · weight: high

3.1.e.2 · weight: high

Explicitly configured by admin. Highly trusted.

3.1.e.3 · weight: high

3.1.e.4 · weight: high

Cisco's IGP. Considered more reliable than OSPF.

Route is NEVER installed. Effectively disables the route.

3.1.e.10 · weight: med

Second number in [AD/metric]. Used to compare routes WITHIN THE SAME protocol.

OSPF external type 2 candidate default. Appears when default learned via OSPF.

3.1.g.5 · weight: med

3.2.b.2 · weight: high

Connected=0, Static=1, eBGP=20, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, IS-IS=115, RIP=120, EIGRP-ext=170, iBGP=200, Unknown=255.

Prefix (LPM), AD, Metric. The 3-step hierarchy. NEVER skip a step or jump ahead.

Used only if Step 1 produces a tie (same mask). Lower AD wins.

3.2.e.3 · weight: high

Used only if Steps 1 AND 2 tie (same mask + same protocol). Lower metric wins. Ties = ECMP.

3.2.e.4 · weight: high

Don't jump to AD before checking prefix length. A /28 from RIP still beats a /24 from connected.

3.2.e.5 · weight: high

'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop]'. Matches any destination. Gateway of last resort.

3.3.a.1 · weight: high

'ipv6 route ::/0 [next-hop]'. Same concept; IPv6 syntax.

3.3.a.2 · weight: high

Single default route to HQ/ISP. No need for full routing table.

3.3.a.3 · weight: med

Static candidate default route marker. Only appears when a default is configured.

3.3.a.4 · weight: high

'ip route [network] [mask] [next-hop|interface]'. Manual route to a specific network.

3.3.b.1 · weight: high

Default AD for static routes. Wins against all dynamic protocols unless AD is manually raised.

3.3.b.2 · weight: high

Need routes in BOTH directions. R1→R3's LAN AND R3→R1's LAN. Missing return route = classic trap.

3.3.b.3 · weight: high

'ipv6 route [prefix/length] [next-hop|interface]'. Same concept.

3.3.b.4 · weight: high

Route for a single IP. Mask 255.255.255.255. Most specific possible — always wins LPM.

3.3.c.1 · weight: high

Router auto-creates /32 local routes for its own interface IPs. Ensures local processing.

3.3.c.2 · weight: high

Force specific traffic through a specific next-hop regardless of broader routes. Policy-based steering.

3.3.c.3 · weight: med

Static route with AD set HIGHER than primary. Stays out of table until primary fails.

3.3.d.1 · weight: high

'ip route [network] [mask] [next-hop] [AD]'. Append AD value at the end of the command.

3.3.d.2 · weight: high

If primary is OSPF (110), set floating static to 111+. Typical: 200-250 for safety margin.

3.3.d.3 · weight: high

Forgetting to append AD leaves static at default 1 → always wins, primary never used. Classic failure.

3.3.d.4 · weight: high

Primary fails → floating static activates. Primary returns → primary reclaims, floating goes back to standby.

3.3.d.5 · weight: high

'ipv6 route [prefix/length] [next-hop|interface]'. Same structure as IPv4.

3.3.e.1 · weight: high

GLOBAL command. Must be enabled or router won't forward IPv6. Most forgotten prerequisite.

3.3.e.2 · weight: high

If next-hop is fe80::..., MUST specify exit interface too: 'ipv6 route ::/0 Gi0/0 fe80::1'.

3.3.e.3 · weight: high

fe80:: addresses are unique per-interface, not globally. Router can't determine exit interface alone.

3.3.e.4 · weight: high

'ipv6 route ::/0 [next-hop]'. ::/0 = match everything. IPv6 default route.

3.3.e.5 · weight: high

Router looks up next-hop in table to find exit interface. Preferred on Ethernet.

3.3.f.1 · weight: high

No recursive lookup. Fine on P2P. Causes ARP flooding on Ethernet (ARP per destination IP).

3.3.f.2 · weight: high

'ip route ... Gi0/0 192.168.1.2'. Best of both worlds. No recursion, no ARP issues.

3.3.f.3 · weight: high

Next-hop IP or fully-specified on Ethernet. Exit-interface-only ONLY on point-to-point.

3.3.f.4 · weight: high

'show running-config | include ip route' — verifies the command was entered.

3.3.g.1 · weight: high

'show ip route static' — verifies route is INSTALLED in the table (not just configured).

3.3.g.2 · weight: high

ping + traceroute — verifies traffic actually reaches the destination end-to-end.

3.3.g.3 · weight: high

Route is in config but not in table = exit interface down OR next-hop unreachable.

3.3.g.4 · weight: high

Missing return route, wrong next-hop, wrong mask, down interface, asymmetric routing.

3.3.g.5 · weight: high

Sent every 10s (broadcast/P2P) or 30s (NBMA). Discover and maintain neighbor relationships.

3.4.a.1 · weight: high

4× Hello by default. 40s (broadcast/P2P) or 120s (NBMA). Neighbor declared down after this.

3.4.a.2 · weight: high

Hello/Dead timers, Area ID, MTU, Subnet mask, Stub flag. ALL must match for adjacency.

3.4.a.3 · weight: high

Down → Init → 2-Way → ExStart → Exchange → Loading → Full. Full = synced, ready.

3.4.a.4 · weight: high

Hellos not arriving. ACL blocking 224.0.0.5/224.0.0.6 or interface issue.

3.4.a.5 · weight: high

MTU mismatch between interfaces. Classic hard-to-diagnose issue.

3.4.a.6 · weight: high

On broadcast networks, DROther-to-DROther stays 2-Way. Full only with DR/BDR. Not a problem.

3.4.a.7 · weight: high

No DR/BDR election needed. Two routers on link go directly to Full adjacency.

3.4.b.1 · weight: high

Interface command to force Ethernet to act as P2P. Skip DR election, faster convergence.

3.4.b.2 · weight: high

show ip ospf neighbor output. Dash = no DR role = P2P network type.

3.4.b.3 · weight: high

10 seconds (default). Dead interval 40 seconds (4×).

3.4.b.4 · weight: med

Elected on broadcast/multi-access networks. Reduces LSA flooding. Highest priority wins.

3.4.c.1 · weight: high

Second-highest priority. Takes over if DR fails.

3.4.c.2 · weight: high

Highest priority wins. Tie → highest Router ID. Priority 0 = never DR.

3.4.c.3 · weight: high

DR stays until it FAILS. Higher priority joining later does NOT trigger new election. Classic trap.

3.4.c.4 · weight: high

'clear ip ospf process' or shutdown DR's interface. Changing priority alone does NOT.

3.4.c.5 · weight: high

Interface command. Sets OSPF priority. Default 1. 0 = opt out of DR/BDR role.

3.4.c.6 · weight: high

AllSPFRouters / AllDRouters multicast. Routers → 224.0.0.5 for Hello. LSAs to DR/BDR on 224.0.0.6.

3.4.c.7 · weight: med

32-bit unique identifier per OSPF router. Formatted like IP address. Must be unique in OSPF domain.

3.4.d.1 · weight: high

Manual 'router-id' > highest Loopback IP > highest active Physical IP.

3.4.d.2 · weight: high

Determined when OSPF process starts. Does NOT auto-update when you change config or add loopbacks.

3.4.d.3 · weight: high

Restart OSPF to apply new RID. Drops all adjacencies temporarily.

3.4.d.4 · weight: high

Best practice. Manual RID is predictable and survives interface changes.

3.4.d.5 · weight: high

Start OSPF process. Process ID is LOCALLY SIGNIFICANT — does NOT need to match between routers.

3.4.e.1 · weight: high

Under OSPF process. Manually set RID. Best practice in production.

3.4.e.2 · weight: high

Traditional method. Wildcard = inverse of subnet mask. Activates OSPF on matching interfaces.

3.4.e.3 · weight: high

Modern method. Per-interface command. No wildcard math needed. Preferred.

3.4.e.4 · weight: high

/24 → 0.0.0.255, /30 → 0.0.0.3, /32 → 0.0.0.0. Practice until instant.

3.4.e.5 · weight: high

Single-area OSPF uses area 0 exclusively. Multi-area is out of CCNA scope.

3.4.e.6 · weight: high

Interface that advertises its network but does NOT send/receive Hellos. No OSPF adjacencies formed.

3.4.f.1 · weight: high

Best practice. Makes ALL interfaces passive, then selectively enable with 'no passive-interface [int]'.

3.4.f.2 · weight: high

Network is still in OSPF database and shared with neighbors. Only Hellos suppressed.

3.4.f.3 · weight: high

If OSPF neighbor won't form on interface that should be active, check if it's accidentally passive.

3.4.f.4 · weight: high

cost = reference BW / interface BW. Sum along path. Lower total = preferred.

3.4.g.1 · weight: high

Problem: 100M and 1G and 10G all calculate to cost 1 (min). Can't differentiate modern speeds.

3.4.g.2 · weight: high

OSPF process command. Set to 10000 (10 Gbps) to differentiate modern links. MUST match on all routers.

3.4.g.3 · weight: high

Interface command. Manually overrides calculated cost. Useful for fine path tuning.

3.4.g.4 · weight: med

Ref 10000: 10M=1000, 100M=100, 1G=10, 10G=1. Memorize.

3.4.g.5 · weight: high

OSPF command. Advertise 0.0.0.0/0 as external type 2 LSA to all OSPF neighbors.

3.4.h.1 · weight: high

Only advertises default if router HAS a default route in its own table first. Safer.

3.4.h.2 · weight: high

Advertises default regardless. Router may advertise even when its own internet path is down. Less safe.

3.4.h.3 · weight: high

How the default appears on receiving routers. OSPF external type 2 candidate default.

3.4.h.4 · weight: high

External type 2 cost stays constant across OSPF domain (unlike E1 which adds internal cost).

3.4.h.5 · weight: med

Primary OSPF verification. State (Full/2-Way/Init/etc.), RID, priority, DR/BDR role, dead timer.

3.4.i.1 · weight: high

Per-interface summary: area, cost, state, neighbor count. Fast overview.

3.4.i.2 · weight: high

Detailed view: timers, network type, DR/BDR IPs, passive status.

3.4.i.3 · weight: high

OSPF process info: RID, area config, SPF stats, reference bandwidth.

3.4.i.4 · weight: high

OSPF-learned routes only. O (intra), O IA (inter-area), O E1/E2 (external).

3.4.i.5 · weight: high

All running routing protocols overview. Quick sanity check for OSPF config.

3.4.i.6 · weight: high

First Hop Redundancy Protocol. Eliminates single point of failure at default gateway.

3.5.a.1 · weight: high

Shared IP that hosts use as default gateway. Active router responds with shared virtual MAC.

3.5.a.2 · weight: high

Shared L2 address. Takes over with VIP on failover. Hosts continue without reconfiguration.

3.5.a.3 · weight: high

HSRP (Cisco, Active/Standby), VRRP (open, Master/Backup), GLBP (Cisco, load-balancing).

3.5.a.4 · weight: high

Hosts never reconfigure gateway. Traffic continues through a different router using the same VIP+MAC.

3.5.a.5 · weight: high

Hot Standby Router Protocol. Cisco proprietary FHRP. Active/Standby terminology.

3.5.b.1 · weight: high

0000.0c07.acXX (v1) where XX = group in hex. Memorize format.

3.5.b.2 · weight: high

UDP port 1985. Multicast 224.0.0.2 (v1) / 224.0.0.102 (v2).

3.5.b.3 · weight: high

Hello 3s, Hold 10s. Dead = 3× Hello.

3.5.b.4 · weight: high

Range 0-255. Higher wins. Ties broken by highest interface IP.

3.5.b.5 · weight: high

Higher-priority router does NOT auto-reclaim Active role. Must enable with 'standby [grp] preempt'.

3.5.b.6 · weight: high

standby [grp] ip [vip], standby [grp] priority [n], standby [grp] preempt.

3.5.b.7 · weight: high

0-4095 (vs v1's 0-255). Supports large VLAN deployments.

3.5.c.1 · weight: med

0000.0c9f.fXXX (where XXX = group number). Different format from v1.

3.5.c.2 · weight: med

224.0.0.102 (IPv4) / ff02::66 (IPv6). Dedicated v2 multicast.

3.5.c.3 · weight: med

v1 is IPv4 only. v2 supports both IPv4 and IPv6.

3.5.c.4 · weight: med

All routers in an HSRP group MUST run the same version. Mixing breaks the group.

3.5.c.5 · weight: med

Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol. Open standard RFC 5798. Master/Backup terminology.

3.5.d.1 · weight: high

Active = Master. Standby = Backup. See these terms → it's VRRP.

3.5.d.2 · weight: high

Opposite of HSRP. Higher-priority router auto-reclaims Master role on return.

3.5.d.3 · weight: high

Front: VRRP virtual IP can match physical
Back:
Unique to VRRP. IP owner automatically has priority 255.

3.5.d.4 · weight: high

IP protocol 112 (not UDP). Multicast 224.0.0.18.

3.5.d.5 · weight: high

1 second (faster than HSRP's 3s). Dead typically 3× advertisement = 3s.

3.5.d.6 · weight: med

1-254 range, default 100. Priority 255 reserved for IP address owner.

3.5.d.7 · weight: med

Gateway Load Balancing Protocol. Cisco proprietary. ONLY FHRP with active-active load balancing.

3.5.e.1 · weight: high

Responds to ARP requests. Hands out different virtual MACs per host to distribute load.

3.5.e.2 · weight: high

Router that forwards traffic for its assigned virtual MAC. Up to 4 AVFs per group.

3.5.e.3 · weight: high

round-robin (default), weighted, host-dependent.

3.5.e.4 · weight: med

0007.b400.XXYY (XX = group, YY = forwarder). UDP 3222, multicast 224.0.0.102.

3.5.e.5 · weight: low

Whether a higher-priority router auto-reclaims Active/Master role after recovery.

3.5.f.1 · weight: high

Returning higher-priority router does NOT reclaim. Must enable: 'standby [grp] preempt'.

3.5.f.2 · weight: high

Returning higher-priority router automatically reclaims Master. Disable with 'no vrrp [grp] preempt'.

3.5.f.3 · weight: high

Preferred router usually has best path (closest to WAN). Preemption ensures it returns to primary role.

3.5.f.4 · weight: high

Scenario: HSRP active fails, returns. Without preempt, it stays as standby. Students often get this wrong.

3.5.f.5 · weight: high

Private IP of host on the inside network. The REAL internal IP. Example: 192.168.1.10.

4.1.a.1 · weight: high

Public IP representing the inside host to the outside. The translated address seen by external hosts.

4.1.a.2 · weight: high

How external host appears to inside network. Usually same as Outside Global.

4.1.a.3 · weight: med

Real public IP of the external host. Example: 8.8.8.8.

4.1.a.4 · weight: med

'Inside' = where the private host lives. 'Local' = the real address. 'Global' = what the internet sees.

4.1.a.5 · weight: high

Permanent one-to-one mapping. Inside Local ↔ Inside Global. Bidirectional. Best for servers.

4.1.b.1 · weight: high

'ip nat inside source static [inside-local] [inside-global]'. Creates the permanent mapping.

4.1.b.2 · weight: high

External traffic TO the public IP is translated to the private IP. Enables externally-initiated connections.

4.1.b.3 · weight: high

Each static mapping consumes a public IP. Expensive for many servers.

4.1.b.4 · weight: med

Pool-based one-to-one mapping. Temporary. Public IP returns to pool after timeout.

4.1.c.1 · weight: high

'ip nat pool NAME [start-ip] [end-ip] netmask [mask]'. Defines available public IPs.

4.1.c.2 · weight: high

When all pool IPs are in use, new connections are DROPPED (not queued).

4.1.c.3 · weight: high

Dynamic = one-to-one temporary. PAT = many-to-one using ports. Difference is the 'overload' keyword.

4.1.c.4 · weight: high

PAT has largely replaced dynamic NAT. Exam still tests dynamic NAT for concept understanding.

4.1.c.5 · weight: med

Many-to-one translation. All inside hosts share ONE public IP. Differentiated by source ports.

4.1.d.1 · weight: high

CRITICAL. Transforms dynamic NAT into PAT. Without it = dynamic NAT (one-to-one).

4.1.d.2 · weight: high

'ip nat inside source list [acl] interface [outside-if] overload'. Uses outside interface's IP as public IP.

4.1.d.3 · weight: high

'ip nat inside source list [acl] pool [name] overload'. Uses pool IPs with overload.

4.1.d.4 · weight: high

Each internal host gets unique source port on shared public IP. ~65,000 sessions per public IP.

4.1.d.5 · weight: high

Every consumer router does PAT. All home devices share one ISP-assigned public IP.

4.1.d.6 · weight: high

Interface command. Designates interface facing private/internal network.

4.1.e.1 · weight: high

Interface command. Designates interface facing public/external network.

4.1.e.2 · weight: high

#1 NAT troubleshooting issue. NAT rules are ignored if interfaces aren't designated.

4.1.e.3 · weight: high

Reveals interface designations under 'Inside/Outside interfaces'. Primary verification tool.

4.1.e.4 · weight: high

Selects which inside hosts get translated. Not a traffic filter — selection filter.

4.1.f.1 · weight: high

Permit = translate this traffic. Different meaning from interface ACLs.

4.1.f.2 · weight: high

Deny = DO NOT translate (but traffic is NOT blocked). Passes through untranslated.

4.1.f.3 · weight: high

NAT source matching only needs source IP. Standard ACL works: 'access-list 1 permit [net] [wildcard]'.

4.1.f.4 · weight: high

View active NAT entries. Columns: Inside Local, Inside Global, Outside Local, Outside Global, Protocol.

4.1.g.1 · weight: high

Overall metrics: totals, hits, misses, pool usage, inside/outside interfaces.

4.1.g.2 · weight: high

Indicates pool exhaustion (dynamic NAT) or ACL mismatch. Check both.

4.1.g.3 · weight: med

PAT translations include source port in output. Same public IP, different ports per host.

4.1.g.4 · weight: high

Flush all dynamic NAT entries. Static entries unaffected. Useful for troubleshooting.

4.1.g.5 · weight: med

(1) Interfaces designated? (2) ACL correct? (3) Pool exhausted? (4) Command syntax right (overload)?

4.1.h.1 · weight: high

Most common NAT failure. Check 'show ip nat statistics' for 'Inside interfaces' and 'Outside interfaces'.

4.1.h.2 · weight: high

Configured PAT but forgot overload = got dynamic NAT. Only works for N hosts where N = pool size.

4.1.h.3 · weight: high

Shows real-time NAT events per packet. High CPU impact — use cautiously.

4.1.h.4 · weight: med

Network Time Protocol. Synchronizes device clocks. UDP port 123. Millisecond accuracy over LAN.

4.2.a.1 · weight: high

Log correlation, certificate validation, Kerberos (within 5 min), compliance audit trails.

4.2.a.2 · weight: high

Time flows from authoritative source (atomic clock) down through stratum levels.

4.2.a.3 · weight: high

Gradual clock adjustment (speeds up or slows down) rather than sudden jumps. Avoids disruption.

4.2.a.4 · weight: med

Reference clock itself (atomic, GPS, radio). Hardware. Not directly reachable over NTP.

4.2.b.1 · weight: high

Directly connected to stratum 0. Most accurate NTP source on the network.

4.2.b.2 · weight: high

Each hop increments stratum by 1. All are valid, usable sources. Lower = more accurate.

4.2.b.3 · weight: high

UNSYNCHRONIZED. Device has no valid time source. Others refuse to sync from it.

4.2.b.4 · weight: high

Multiple sources? Client picks lowest stratum (closest to reference clock).

4.2.b.5 · weight: high

Client command. Sync the local clock to the specified NTP server. Can specify multiple.

4.2.c.1 · weight: high

Configure 2+ for redundancy. Device selects the best source based on stratum, delay, jitter.

4.2.c.2 · weight: med

Lists configured sources. '*' = selected. '+' = candidate. 'reach 377' = all 8 recent polls succeeded.

4.2.c.3 · weight: high

Shows 'Clock is synchronized/unsynchronized', stratum, and reference clock ID.

4.2.c.4 · weight: high

server = client-only. peer = symmetric (both give and receive time). Use server for CCNA scenarios.

4.2.c.5 · weight: med

Router becomes NTP server using its own clock as reference. Advertises given stratum value.

4.2.d.1 · weight: high

master = I PROVIDE time. server = I RECEIVE time. A router can be both simultaneously.

4.2.d.2 · weight: high

If master advertises stratum 5, clients syncing to it become stratum 6. Each hop adds 1.

4.2.d.3 · weight: high

Router's local clock is not atomic-accurate. Use stratum 5-8 to avoid outranking real NTP servers.

4.2.d.4 · weight: med

Prevents rogue NTP server attacks. Client only trusts servers with matching auth key.

4.2.e.1 · weight: med

Global command. Enables NTP authentication on the device.

4.2.e.2 · weight: med

Defines an auth key with MD5 hash. Key number + password must match on both sides.

4.2.e.3 · weight: med

Marks a key as trusted. Client only syncs from servers using trusted keys.

4.2.e.4 · weight: med

Client command. Associates a specific key with a specific NTP server.

4.2.e.5 · weight: med

List of configured NTP sources, their stratums, and reach. '*' = selected, '+' = candidate.

4.2.f.1 · weight: high

Shows sync state: 'Clock is synchronized' or 'unsynchronized'. Stratum, reference ID.

4.2.f.2 · weight: high

All 8 recent polls succeeded. Binary 11111111 = perfect reachability.

4.2.f.3 · weight: med

If show ntp status shows stratum 16, device is NOT synced. Troubleshoot immediately.

4.2.f.4 · weight: high

Discover → Offer → Request → Acknowledge. Four-step DHCP IP assignment sequence.

4.3.a.1 · weight: high

Client broadcasts 'anyone have an IP?' (dest 255.255.255.255, src 0.0.0.0). Client UDP 68 → server UDP 67.

4.3.a.2 · weight: high

Server broadcasts offer with proposed IP, mask, gateway, DNS, lease time. Server UDP 67 → client UDP 68.

4.3.a.3 · weight: high

Client broadcasts 'I'll take this offer.' Tells other DHCP servers to withdraw. Required so other servers free their offers.

4.3.a.4 · weight: high

Server confirms the lease. Client now uses the IP. Lease timer starts.

4.3.a.5 · weight: high

Configured on a SVI/interface. Forwards DHCP broadcasts as unicast to DHCP server across subnets.

4.3.a.6 · weight: high

Router/L3 switch acts as relay because broadcasts don't cross L3 boundaries. 'ip helper-address' does this.

4.3.a.7 · weight: high

Create a DHCP pool. Enters DHCP pool config mode.

4.3.b.1 · weight: high

Specify the subnet the pool serves. Under pool config.

4.3.b.2 · weight: high

Gateway IP sent to clients. IMPORTANT: 'default-router' (not 'default-gateway'). Classic exam trap.

4.3.b.3 · weight: high

DNS server(s) sent to clients in the offer.

4.3.b.4 · weight: high

Lease duration. Default 24 hours. Longer leases reduce DHCP traffic but slow reclaiming.

4.3.b.5 · weight: med

Global command (OUTSIDE pool config). Reserves IPs so DHCP won't assign them (e.g., gateway, servers).

4.3.b.6 · weight: high

At 50% of lease, client unicasts DHCPREQUEST to original server for renewal.

4.3.c.1 · weight: high

At 87.5% of lease, if T1 failed, client broadcasts DHCPREQUEST to ANY DHCP server.

4.3.c.2 · weight: high

If lease expires without renewal, client releases IP and restarts DORA.

4.3.c.3 · weight: med

T1 = unicast (direct to original server). T2 = broadcast (any server can help).

4.3.c.4 · weight: high

Hostname → IPv4 address. Most common DNS record. example.com → 93.184.216.34.

4.3.d.1 · weight: high

Hostname → IPv6 address. The IPv6 equivalent of an A record.

4.3.d.2 · weight: high

Canonical name (alias). Maps one name to another name. www.example.com → example.com.

4.3.d.3 · weight: high

Mail Exchange. Specifies email server(s) for a domain. Includes priority value.

4.3.d.4 · weight: high

Pointer (reverse DNS). Maps IP → hostname. Used for reverse lookups.

4.3.d.5 · weight: high

Name Server. Specifies authoritative DNS servers for a zone.

4.3.d.6 · weight: med

Start of Authority. Metadata about a DNS zone (primary server, admin email, serial, timers).

4.3.d.7 · weight: med

Client expects the DNS server to return the FINAL answer. Server does all the work.

4.3.e.1 · weight: high

DNS server may return a REFERRAL (another server to ask). Caller keeps asking.

4.3.e.2 · weight: high

Recursive to local DNS resolver. Resolver then does iterative queries up the hierarchy.

4.3.e.3 · weight: high

Resolvers cache answers based on TTL. Reduces repeated full lookups and load on authoritative servers.

4.3.e.4 · weight: med

Root → TLD (.com, .org, .net) → domain (example.com) → host (www.example.com).

4.3.f.1 · weight: high

A through M. Globally distributed (anycast). Resolution starts at the root.

4.3.f.2 · weight: med

.com, .org, .net, .io, country codes. Managed by specific registries.

4.3.f.3 · weight: med

Fully Qualified Domain Name. Full name including all levels: 'www.example.com.' (trailing dot = root).

4.3.f.4 · weight: low

Simple Network Management Protocol. Monitor and manage network devices. UDP only (not TCP).

4.4.a.1 · weight: high

The management station (e.g., SolarWinds, PRTG). Sends queries and receives traps.

4.4.a.2 · weight: high

Runs on managed device (router/switch). Responds to manager queries. Sends traps on events.

4.4.a.3 · weight: high

Hierarchical database of manageable objects (OIDs) on a device. Agent exposes data via MIB.

4.4.a.4 · weight: high

161 = manager queries (GET/SET to agent). 162 = agent traps/informs (to manager).

4.4.a.5 · weight: high

Manager → Agent queries to retrieve OID values. GetBulk = multiple OIDs in one query (v2c+).

4.4.b.1 · weight: high

Manager → Agent command to MODIFY a value. Requires read-write community/user.

4.4.b.2 · weight: high

Agent → Manager unsolicited alert. UDP 162. NOT acknowledged — fire-and-forget (unreliable).

4.4.b.3 · weight: high

Agent → Manager alert with ACK required. Reliable version of trap. Retransmitted if no ACK.

4.4.b.4 · weight: high

Both agent-initiated on UDP 162. Trap = fire-and-forget. Inform = reliable (ACK-based).

4.4.b.5 · weight: high

Plaintext passwords for SNMP access. Two types: read-only (RO) and read-write (RW). Case-sensitive.

4.4.c.1 · weight: high

Historic default read-only community string. 'public' / 'private' are textbook defaults — never use in production.

4.4.c.2 · weight: high

Both plaintext community strings. v2c adds bulk operations (GetBulk) and Inform. v1 is basically obsolete.

4.4.c.3 · weight: med

Cisco command to define community string and access level.

4.4.c.4 · weight: med

Adds authentication AND encryption to SNMP. RECOMMENDED for production. Only secure SNMP version.

4.4.d.1 · weight: high

No authentication, no encryption. Uses username only. Weakest SNMPv3 level.

4.4.d.2 · weight: high

Authentication (MD5/SHA) but no encryption. Prevents spoofing but traffic is plaintext.

4.4.d.3 · weight: high

BOTH authentication AND encryption. Strongest level. Auth = MD5/SHA. Encryption = DES/3DES/AES.

4.4.d.4 · weight: high

Auth: MD5, SHA (SHA is preferred). Encryption: DES (weak), 3DES, AES-128/192/256.

4.4.d.5 · weight: med

Plaintext community strings. No auth, no encryption. Effectively obsolete.

4.4.e.1 · weight: med

Same as v1 (plaintext communities) but adds GetBulk and Inform messages.

4.4.e.2 · weight: high

Username-based auth + optional encryption. noAuthNoPriv / authNoPriv / authPriv levels.

4.4.e.3 · weight: high

ONLY SNMPv3 (at authPriv level). v1 and v2c are always plaintext.

4.4.e.4 · weight: high

0 Emergency, 1 Alert, 2 Critical, 3 Error, 4 Warning, 5 Notification, 6 Informational, 7 Debug.

4.5.a.1 · weight: high

'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily.' E-A-C-E-W-N-I-D = 0-1-2-3-4-5-6-7.

4.5.a.2 · weight: high

0 = most critical (emergency). 7 = most verbose (debug). Severity INVERSELY related to number.

4.5.a.3 · weight: high

Captures that level AND MORE SEVERE (lower). trap 4 = levels 0-4 (emergency through warning).

4.5.a.4 · weight: high

Destination port for syslog messages. Remember: UDP (not TCP), port 514.

4.5.a.5 · weight: high

System unusable. Device about to crash or panic. Action required immediately.

4.5.b.1 · weight: med

Immediate action needed. Something critical happened but device may still operate.

4.5.b.2 · weight: med

Critical condition. Serious but device still runs. Example: hardware temperature critical.

4.5.c.1 · weight: med

%FACILITY-SEVERITY-MNEMONIC: message. Example: %LINK-3-UPDOWN. Middle number = severity.

4.5.c.2 · weight: high

Error condition. Something failed but device recovers. Most daily troubleshooting lives here.

4.5.d.1 · weight: med

OSPF neighbor down, interface errors, authentication failures. Common real-world logs.

4.5.d.2 · weight: low

Warning condition. Potentially problematic but not yet an error. Example: config changed.

4.5.e.1 · weight: med

Sends levels 0-4 (Emergency through Warning) to syslog server. Common production setting.

4.5.e.2 · weight: high

Normal but significant event. OSPF adjacency up/down, interface state change, config save.

4.5.f.1 · weight: med

%LINK-5-CHANGED (interface state), %OSPF-5-ADJCHG (OSPF neighbor state).

4.5.f.2 · weight: low

Informational message. Routine events. Classic trap: Notification=5, Informational=6. Don't swap them.

4.5.g.1 · weight: med

Notification (5) is more significant than Informational (6). Mnemonic has N before I.

4.5.g.2 · weight: med

Least severe, MOST verbose. Enable only for troubleshooting. Never leave on in production — floods logs.

4.5.h.1 · weight: high

'debug ...' at privileged exec. Generates level 7 messages. Turn off with 'no debug all' or 'undebug all'.

4.5.h.2 · weight: med

Front: 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily'
Back:
Mnemonic for 0-7: Emergency, Alert, Critical, Error, Warning, Notification, Informational, Debug.

4.5.i.1 · weight: high

Write syslog severities + AD values + subnet chart FIRST at exam start. Frees mental space for questions.

4.5.i.2 · weight: high

Console, VTY terminal, buffer (RAM), syslog server. Each has its own severity filter.

4.5.j.1 · weight: high

'logging console [level]'. Default enabled at level 7. Seen on direct console connection.

4.5.j.2 · weight: high

'logging monitor [level]' + 'terminal monitor' per session. Seen over SSH/Telnet.

4.5.j.3 · weight: med

'logging buffered [level]'. Stored in RAM. View with 'show logging'. Lost on reboot.

4.5.j.4 · weight: high

'logging [server-ip]' + 'logging trap [level]'. Survives reboot. UDP 514. Best for production.

4.5.j.5 · weight: high

Set syslog server destination. Required first step for server logging.

4.5.k.1 · weight: high

Filter messages sent to syslog server. Captures that level + all more severe (lower numbers).

4.5.k.2 · weight: high

Best practice. Adds date/time to every log message. Critical for correlation.

4.5.k.3 · weight: high

Adds sequence numbers to log messages. Helps detect dropped messages.

4.5.k.4 · weight: low
