Interface command. Makes the router interface a DHCP CLIENT. Gets IP from a DHCP server.

4.6.a.1 · weight: high

Used for WAN interface facing ISP. ISP runs DHCP; router gets public IP dynamically.

4.6.a.2 · weight: high

Interface command. Forwards DHCP broadcasts as unicast to the server IP.

4.6.b.1 · weight: high

Apply to the interface that the client uses (the router's LAN-side SVI, not the server-side).

4.6.b.2 · weight: high

The IP you enter in 'ip helper-address' is the DHCP server's address, not the client's.

4.6.b.3 · weight: high

Routers drop broadcasts by default. DHCP Discover is a broadcast — won't reach server on another subnet.

4.6.c.1 · weight: high

Router converts client broadcast to unicast to the DHCP server. Essential for centralized DHCP.

4.6.c.2 · weight: high

One DHCP server serves all subnets. Each router with a client-facing SVI has 'ip helper-address'.

4.6.c.3 · weight: med

1) exclude gateway/static IPs, 2) pool NAME, 3) network, 4) default-router, 5) dns-server, 6) lease.

4.6.d.1 · weight: high

Exclude BEFORE pool is safest (excluded IPs never leased). IOS accepts either order but practice best.

4.6.d.2 · weight: med

Full DHCP pool support built in. Can serve multiple subnets (multiple pools).

4.6.d.3 · weight: high

GLOBAL command (NOT inside pool config). Reserves IPs so DHCP won't assign them.

4.6.e.1 · weight: high

The router's own gateway IP is usually at .1. Must exclude or DHCP could assign it to a client (conflict).

4.6.e.2 · weight: high

'ip dhcp excluded-address 10.0.0.1 10.0.0.10' reserves the whole range. Supports single or range.

4.6.e.3 · weight: med

Shows active DHCP leases: IP, client MAC, lease expiration. Exam often reads from this output.

4.6.f.1 · weight: high

Shows pool stats: total addresses, leased, excluded.

4.6.f.2 · weight: med

Shows IP conflict detection. If two devices claim same IP, DHCP logs it here.

4.6.f.3 · weight: low

Classification → Marking → Queuing → Policing/Shaping → Congestion avoidance. Memorize the order.

4.7.a.1 · weight: high

Identify traffic type (voice, video, data). Based on ACL, protocol, port, or deep packet inspection.

4.7.a.2 · weight: high

Tag frames/packets with QoS value (CoS, DSCP). Subsequent hops use the mark to prioritize.

4.7.a.3 · weight: high

Each router independently applies QoS based on the marking. End-to-end consistency requires all routers to agree.

4.7.a.4 · weight: med

No config tested. Know terminology, order, and what each component does.

4.7.a.5 · weight: high

L2 marking. 3 bits in 802.1Q tag (PCP field). 8 values (0-7). Lost at L3 boundaries.

4.7.b.1 · weight: high

L3 marking. 6 bits in IP header (ToS field). 64 values. SURVIVES across L3 hops.

4.7.b.2 · weight: high

CoS = L2 only. DSCP = L3, end-to-end. For end-to-end QoS across routed networks, use DSCP.

4.7.b.3 · weight: high

Older L3 marking. 3 bits (same position as CoS but in IP header). Replaced by DSCP.

4.7.b.4 · weight: med

DSCP value 46. Used for VOICE traffic (VoIP). Lowest latency, strict priority.

4.7.c.1 · weight: high

4 classes × 3 drop precedences (12 values). Used for video and data classes. AF41 = high priority video.

4.7.c.2 · weight: med

Backward compatibility with IP Precedence. CS0-CS7. First 3 DSCP bits mirror IPP values.

4.7.c.3 · weight: med

Best-effort. Untagged or default traffic. Gets whatever bandwidth remains after priority queues.

4.7.c.4 · weight: med

Strict priority queue. Used for voice. Always served first. Has policing to prevent starving other queues.

4.7.d.1 · weight: high

Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing. Guarantees minimum bandwidth per class. Used for video and data.

4.7.d.2 · weight: high

First-In-First-Out. Default queuing. No prioritization. Large packets delay small ones.

4.7.d.3 · weight: med

Simple queue-full behavior — new packets dropped. Can cause TCP global synchronization.

4.7.d.4 · weight: med

Weighted Random Early Detection. Drops packets proactively BEFORE queue is full. Selective by class.

4.7.e.1 · weight: high

Problem WRED solves. Many TCP flows hit tail drop simultaneously → all back off in unison → bandwidth wasted.

4.7.e.2 · weight: high

WRED = proactive, selective, class-aware. Tail drop = reactive, indiscriminate, at-the-brink.

4.7.e.3 · weight: high

DROPS or re-marks excess traffic. Hard limit. Bursty output. Can be applied at ingress OR egress.

4.7.f.1 · weight: high

BUFFERS excess traffic and releases at a smooth rate. Soft limit. Egress only. Adds latency.

4.7.f.2 · weight: high

Policing drops/remarks. Shaping buffers/delays. Policing = strict, shaping = gentle.

4.7.f.3 · weight: high

Buffering = delay. BAD for voice/video. Use on WAN links that aggregate traffic.

4.7.g.1 · weight: high

Drops immediately. Good for enforcing strict caps. OK for voice (drops bad voice packets fast).

4.7.g.2 · weight: high

Shaping: egress WAN links (burst smoothing). Policing: rate-limit enforcement on ingress or egress.

4.7.g.3 · weight: med

Demarcation where the network starts trusting incoming QoS markings. Usually the access-layer switch.

4.7.h.1 · weight: high

As close to source as possible. Access switches mark traffic; core just honors markings.

4.7.h.2 · weight: high

Untrusted endpoints could mark their own traffic as EF (voice priority) and abuse QoS. Boundary prevents that.

4.7.h.3 · weight: high

1) hostname, 2) domain-name, 3) RSA key, 4) user, 5) VTY config, 6) version 2.

4.8.a.1 · weight: high

Miss any one → SSH fails. Exam lab sim = memorize sequence cold.

4.8.a.2 · weight: high

Both required BEFORE RSA key generation. RSA key name = hostname.domain-name.

4.8.b.1 · weight: high

If hostname is still 'Router' (default), key generation fails or produces weak key. Change hostname first.

4.8.b.2 · weight: high

Generates 2048-bit RSA key. Size 2048 is exam answer (vs. weaker 1024 or 768).

4.8.c.1 · weight: high

Technical minimum. But exam/production answer is 2048 bits for security.

4.8.c.2 · weight: med

Global command. Forces SSHv2 only. Rejects insecure SSHv1.

4.8.d.1 · weight: high

SSHv1 has known vulnerabilities. V2 uses stronger crypto and is the standard today.

4.8.d.2 · weight: med

Creates local user with HASHED password (MD5/SCRYPT). Use THIS, not 'password'.

4.8.e.1 · weight: high

'secret' = hashed (secure). 'password' = Type 7 reversible encoding (weak). Always use 'secret' on exam.

4.8.e.2 · weight: high

Enters config for ALL 16 VTY lines. Modern best practice (was 'line vty 0 4' on older IOS).

4.8.f.1 · weight: high

Restricts VTY to SSH only. Rejects Telnet. Critical for security.

4.8.f.2 · weight: high

Uses local username/secret database. Alternative: 'login authentication default' for AAA.

4.8.f.3 · weight: high

'all' allows Telnet AND SSH. 'ssh' rejects Telnet. Always use 'transport input ssh' in secure configs.

4.8.g.1 · weight: high

Telnet transmits credentials IN CLEARTEXT. Anyone sniffing sees passwords. Never use in production.

4.8.g.2 · weight: high

Shows SSH version, auth timeout, retry count. Verifies SSH is enabled and configured.

4.8.h.1 · weight: high

Shows currently active SSH connections: who is connected, version, encryption.

4.8.h.2 · weight: med

Trivial FTP. UDP 69. NO authentication. Simple. Used for IOS images and config transfer.

4.9.a.1 · weight: high

File Transfer Protocol. TCP 20 (data) + 21 (control). HAS authentication (username/password). Cleartext credentials.

4.9.a.2 · weight: high

Secure Copy. TCP 22 (uses SSH). Encrypted transfer WITH authentication. Most secure file transfer.

4.9.a.3 · weight: high

Control channel — login, commands (ls, get, put). Session stays open throughout.

4.9.b.1 · weight: high

Data channel in ACTIVE mode. Server connects back to client on this port to transfer files.

4.9.b.2 · weight: high

FTP has auth but no encryption. Username/password visible on wire. Use SFTP/SCP in production.

4.9.b.3 · weight: high

'copy [source]: [destination]:'. Colons required. Examples: 'copy tftp: flash:', 'copy running-config startup-config'.

4.9.c.1 · weight: high

tftp:, ftp:, scp:, flash:, running-config, startup-config, nvram:. Standard IOS file system locations.

4.9.c.2 · weight: high

Classic use case. 'copy tftp: flash:' transfers new IOS. TFTP chosen for simplicity (no auth needed).

4.9.d.1 · weight: high

Exam answer to 'most secure file transfer'. Uses SSH for auth + encryption.

4.9.d.2 · weight: high

TFTP (UDP 69, no auth) → FTP (TCP 21/20, plain auth) → SCP (TCP 22, encrypted + auth).

4.9.d.3 · weight: high

'copy tftp: running-config' MERGES incoming config with current. Doesn't delete existing commands.

4.9.e.1 · weight: high

'copy tftp: startup-config' REPLACES startup-config entirely. Changes apply after reboot.

4.9.e.2 · weight: high

Lists contents of flash memory. Used to verify IOS image after 'copy tftp: flash:'.

4.9.e.3 · weight: high

Uses SSH for auth + encryption. Most secure file transfer. TCP 22 (same as SSH).

4.9.f.1 · weight: high

Both use SSH. SFTP = file management protocol (rich operations). SCP = simple copy only.

4.9.f.2 · weight: med

Cisco router command to enable SCP server mode. Allows clients to push config/images via SCP.

4.9.f.3 · weight: low

Potential danger/actor. The WHO or WHAT — hacker, malware, insider, disaster.

5.1.a.1 · weight: high

External (hackers, nation-state), internal (employees), environmental (disasters), accidental (human error).

5.1.a.2 · weight: med

Threat exploits Vulnerability using Exploit → Impact. Know each term's role.

5.1.a.3 · weight: high

Weakness/flaw (passive). Exists until mitigated. NOT the attacker — the hole the attacker uses.

5.1.b.1 · weight: high

Software (unpatched), config (defaults, open ports), design (weak crypto), human (weak passwords), physical (open rooms).

5.1.b.2 · weight: high

Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact. Reducing vulnerabilities is the most controllable factor.

5.1.b.3 · weight: med

The technique/tool/code to take advantage of a vulnerability. ACTION, not weakness.

5.1.c.1 · weight: high

Most commonly cited exploit. Send more data than buffer holds → overwrite memory → execute malicious code.

5.1.c.2 · weight: high

Attack against a vulnerability with NO patch available. Extremely dangerous — no defense exists yet.

5.1.c.3 · weight: high

Web-app exploits. SQL: inject SQL commands via form inputs. XSS: inject scripts executed by other users' browsers.

5.1.c.4 · weight: med

Action reducing likelihood/impact of threat. Reduces risk to acceptable level — doesn't eliminate.

5.1.d.1 · weight: high

Preventive (firewall, ACL), Detective (IDS, syslog), Corrective (backup, IR), Deterrent (banner, cameras).

5.1.d.2 · weight: high

Dynamic ARP Inspection uses DHCP snooping binding table to validate ARP replies.

5.1.d.3 · weight: high

Only trusted ports (toward legitimate server) can send DHCP offers. Blocks rogue servers.

5.1.d.4 · weight: high

Technology can't fully defend. User awareness training is the primary countermeasure.

5.1.d.5 · weight: high

Manipulating humans to reveal credentials or bypass security. Bypasses technical controls entirely.

5.1.e.1 · weight: high

Email deception attack. Fake login pages, malicious attachments, urgency tactics.

5.1.e.2 · weight: high

Targeted phishing aimed at specific individual/org. More research, more convincing than bulk phishing.

5.1.e.3 · weight: med

Primary defense against social engineering. Technology alone cannot stop it.

5.1.e.4 · weight: high

Denial of Service. Overwhelms target from a single source. Blockable by ACL or firewall rule.

5.1.f.1 · weight: high

Distributed DoS. Many sources (botnet) attack simultaneously. Needs scrubbing centers/CDN defense.

5.1.f.2 · weight: high

Flood TCP SYNs without completing handshake. Target's half-open connection queue exhausts.

5.1.f.3 · weight: high

Attacker spoofs victim's IP in queries to open servers. Servers reply to victim with large responses.

5.1.f.4 · weight: med

Attacker inserts between two communicating parties, intercepting and/or modifying traffic.

5.1.g.1 · weight: high

Attacker sends forged ARP replies. Hosts ARP-cache the attacker's MAC for other IPs. Traffic flows through attacker.

5.1.g.2 · weight: high

Rogue DHCP server gives victims attacker's IP as gateway/DNS. Attacker becomes MITM for all outbound traffic.

5.1.g.3 · weight: high

DAI (stops ARP spoof), DHCP snooping (stops rogue DHCP), HTTPS/VPN (encrypts even if intercepted).

5.1.g.4 · weight: high

Malware that attaches to legit files. Requires USER ACTION (open file) to spread.

5.1.h.1 · weight: high

Self-propagating malware. Spreads AUTOMATICALLY across network without user action. More dangerous than virus.

5.1.h.2 · weight: high

Malware disguised as legitimate software. Relies on DECEPTION. User installs willingly.

5.1.h.3 · weight: high

Encrypts victim's files, demands payment for decryption key. Major modern threat.

5.1.h.4 · weight: high

Spyware = steals data silently. Adware = pushes unwanted ads. Usually less destructive than ransomware.

5.1.h.5 · weight: med

General security education for all users. Phishing recognition, password hygiene, acceptable use.

5.2.a.1 · weight: high

Social engineering bypasses technology. Trained users are the primary defense.

5.2.a.2 · weight: high

Organizations send fake phishing emails to test users. Those who click get additional training.

5.2.a.3 · weight: med

Technical/role-based skill building. IT admins, incident responders. Deeper than awareness.

5.2.b.1 · weight: high

Awareness = general users (recognize phishing). Training = IT/technical staff (how to respond to incidents).

5.2.b.2 · weight: high

Two-door airlock. One opens only after the other closes. Prevents tailgating. Cisco's preferred modern term.

5.2.c.1 · weight: high

Unauthorized person follows authorized person through access-controlled door. Security vestibule stops this.

5.2.c.2 · weight: high

Physical ID cards (prox, smartcard). Common primary physical auth control.

5.2.c.3 · weight: med

Detective + deterrent control. Records evidence, discourages casual wrongdoing. Doesn't physically stop anything.

5.2.d.1 · weight: low

Detective control. Triggers alert/recording when movement detected. Used in secure areas after hours.

5.2.d.2 · weight: low

Physical controls protecting hardware. Rack locks, data hall cages. Physical category (not logical/technical).

5.2.e.1 · weight: low

Secure laptops/equipment to fixed objects. Low-tech but effective anti-theft.

5.2.e.2 · weight: low

Sets priv-exec password with HASH (Type 5 MD5 or Type 8/9). Preferred. Cannot be reversed.

5.3.a.1 · weight: high

LEGACY. Stores password in plaintext (Type 0) unless service password-encryption. WEAK.

5.3.a.2 · weight: high

If both 'enable secret' and 'enable password' are set, 'secret' is used. 'password' is ignored.

5.3.a.3 · weight: high

Unencrypted in running config. Visible with 'show running-config'. Avoid in production.

5.3.a.4 · weight: high

Plaintext. No encryption. Visible in running-config. AVOID.

5.3.b.1 · weight: high

MD5 hash. One-way, but MD5 is now considered weak by modern standards.

5.3.b.2 · weight: high

Vigenère cipher. REVERSIBLE — can be decrypted easily with online tools. Only applied by 'service password-encryption'.

5.3.b.3 · weight: high

PBKDF2 with SHA-256. Strong modern hash. Use when available.

5.3.b.4 · weight: high

scrypt. Strongest current Cisco password hash. Memory-hard, resistant to GPU attacks.

5.3.b.5 · weight: high

line console 0 → password [pw] → login. Critical: 'login' is required or password is NOT enforced.

5.3.c.1 · weight: high

Password set but no 'login' = password is ignored. Classic exam trap question.

5.3.c.2 · weight: high

Under line console. Prevents log messages from interrupting commands being typed. QoL setting.

5.3.c.3 · weight: med

Auto-logout after N minutes idle. 'exec-timeout 10 0' = 10 min 0 sec. Security best practice.

5.3.c.4 · weight: med

line vty 0 15 → password [pw] → login (or login local). Password protects remote access.

5.3.d.1 · weight: high

If VTY has no password configured, remote login is REJECTED by default on IOS. Access denied.

5.3.d.2 · weight: high

Apply ACL to VTY to restrict which IPs can connect: 'access-class [ACL] in'. Use for management IP restrictions.

5.3.d.3 · weight: high

access-class = VTY lines. access-group = physical interfaces. Don't confuse them.

5.3.d.4 · weight: high

Auxiliary port. Legacy dial-in modem access to router. Rarely used today. 'line aux 0' to configure.

5.3.e.1 · weight: low

Best practice if AUX unused. Prevents anyone plugging in from getting exec prompt.

5.3.e.2 · weight: low

Creates local user with hashed password. Preferred over 'username X password Y' (plaintext).

5.3.f.1 · weight: high

On line (VTY/console) — activates local user database. Without it, local usernames are ignored.

5.3.f.2 · weight: high

Max privilege level. 'username admin privilege 15 secret X' gives instant enable-mode access on login.

5.3.f.3 · weight: high

Applies Type 7 (Vigenère — REVERSIBLE) to plaintext passwords. Not real security — only obscures casual viewing.

5.3.g.1 · weight: high

Vigenère cipher. Reversible with online Cisco Type 7 decoders. Same password always produces same cipher.

5.3.g.2 · weight: high

Use 'enable secret' and 'username X secret Y'. Both use one-way hashing, can't be reversed.

5.3.g.3 · weight: high

MOTD → Login banner → password prompt → EXEC banner. MOTD first, EXEC last (post-login).

5.3.h.1 · weight: high

Should contain LEGAL NOTICE (unauthorized access prohibited). NEVER 'Welcome' or device info.

5.3.h.2 · weight: high

Delimiter character (commonly #) surrounds text. Shown before login prompt.

5.3.h.3 · weight: med

Global command. Enforces minimum length for NEW passwords. Does NOT affect existing passwords.

5.3.i.1 · weight: high

Only applies to passwords configured AFTER the command. Existing weak passwords remain.

5.3.i.2 · weight: med

Global command. Throttles login attempts. Syntax: 'login block-for [sec] attempts [N] within [sec]'.

5.3.j.1 · weight: high

'login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60' = block login for 120s after 3 failed attempts in 60s.

5.3.j.2 · weight: high

block-for THEN attempts THEN within. Memorize the exact order — exam tests this.

5.3.j.3 · weight: high

Mix of upper/lower case, numbers, symbols. IOS doesn't enforce; AAA servers (ISE) do.

5.4.a.1 · weight: high

Longer = stronger. IOS enforces with 'security passwords min-length'. Longer+complex = strongest.

5.4.a.2 · weight: high

Modern NIST recommends long passphrases over complex short passwords. Length beats complexity.

5.4.a.3 · weight: med

Old guidance: change passwords every 60-90 days. Widely enforced by compliance standards.

5.4.b.1 · weight: med

Only change when compromised. Frequent rotation leads to weak patterns (Password1, Password2...).

5.4.b.2 · weight: med

Prevents reuse of last N passwords. Enforced on AAA server or OS (not usually IOS).

5.4.b.3 · weight: low

Authentication using 2+ factors from DIFFERENT categories. Something you know + have + are.

5.4.c.1 · weight: high

Know (password, PIN, security question). Have (token, phone, smartcard). Are (biometric).

5.4.c.2 · weight: high

Password + PIN = NOT MFA (both 'know'). Password + security question = NOT MFA. Must be different categories.

5.4.c.3 · weight: high

MFA requires factors from DIFFERENT categories. Not just 'two things' — specifically different types.

5.4.d.1 · weight: high

Password + TOTP token (know + have). Fingerprint + card (are + have). Password + fingerprint (know + are).

5.4.d.2 · weight: high

Electronic document binding a public key to an identity. Signed by a CA. Used for HTTPS, SSL/TLS, IPsec.

5.4.e.1 · weight: high

Trusted third party that signs certificates. Chain of trust: root CA → intermediate CA → end-entity cert.

5.4.e.2 · weight: high

Subject (owner), Issuer (CA), public key, validity (not-before/not-after), serial number, signature.

5.4.e.3 · weight: high

Public Key Infrastructure. Framework for issuing, distributing, and revoking digital certificates.

5.4.e.4 · weight: med

'Something you ARE.' Fingerprint, iris, facial recognition, voice. Physical characteristics.

5.4.f.1 · weight: high

CANNOT BE CHANGED if compromised. Unlike passwords, you can't reset your fingerprint.

5.4.f.2 · weight: high

FAR = False Accept Rate (bad). FRR = False Reject Rate. CER = Crossover Error Rate (FAR=FRR, tuning point).

5.4.f.3 · weight: low

Virtual Private Network. Creates a secure encrypted tunnel across an untrusted network (internet).

5.5.a.1 · weight: high

Always-on router-to-router (or firewall-to-firewall). Connects entire networks.

5.5.a.2 · weight: high

On-demand client-to-router. Individual user VPN (AnyConnect, OpenVPN, WireGuard).

5.5.a.3 · weight: high

Remote access VPN. Single user needs access from anywhere. AnyConnect client connects to HQ.

5.5.b.1 · weight: high

Site-to-site VPN. Branch office connects to HQ continuously. Users don't know VPN exists.

5.5.b.2 · weight: high

Remote access via browser HTTPS. No VPN client software required. Limited to web-based apps.

5.5.b.3 · weight: med

Data CAN'T BE READ by eavesdroppers. Provided by encryption (AES, 3DES, DES).

5.5.c.1 · weight: high

Data HAS NOT BEEN MODIFIED in transit. Provided by HMAC hashing (MD5, SHA).

5.5.c.2 · weight: high

Confirms peer IDENTITY. Provided by pre-shared keys (PSK) or digital certificates.

5.5.c.3 · weight: high

Prevents attacker from re-sending captured packets. Sequence numbers detect replays.

5.5.c.4 · weight: med

IP protocol 51. Provides integrity + auth BUT NOT encryption. Authenticates entire packet including IP header.

5.5.d.1 · weight: high

AH authenticates the IP header. NAT changes the IP header → AH breaks. Can't use AH through NAT.

5.5.d.2 · weight: high

IP protocol 51, not TCP/UDP. Memorize.

5.5.d.3 · weight: high

IP protocol 50. Provides encryption + integrity + auth. Standard IPsec choice in modern VPNs.

5.5.e.1 · weight: high

IP protocol 50. AH = 51. ESP is the one with encryption.

5.5.e.2 · weight: high

ESP doesn't include IP header in authentication. NAT traversal (UDP 4500) makes it work across NAT.

5.5.e.3 · weight: high

Use ESP (encryption). AH only provides integrity. Modern deployments use ESP exclusively.

5.5.e.4 · weight: high

Authenticate peers, build bidirectional management (ISAKMP) SA. Happens first.

5.5.f.1 · weight: high

Negotiate IPsec SAs (one per direction) for data traffic. Uses the Phase 1 tunnel to protect negotiation.

5.5.f.2 · weight: high

Phase 1 ALWAYS first. Phase 2 can't happen without Phase 1 because it negotiates inside the P1 tunnel.

5.5.f.3 · weight: high

ISAKMP SA. Used only for Phase 2 negotiation. Not for user data.

5.5.g.1 · weight: high

IPsec SAs. One for inbound, one for outbound. Protect actual data traffic.

5.5.g.2 · weight: high

Phase 2 negotiation mode. Faster than Phase 1 because it reuses the P1 tunnel.

5.5.g.3 · weight: med

Entire original packet (incl. IP header) encrypted and wrapped in new IP header. Hides original IPs. Site-to-site default.

5.5.h.1 · weight: high

Only payload encrypted. Original IP header preserved. Used host-to-host (end-to-end, not gateway-to-gateway).

5.5.h.2 · weight: high

TUNNEL mode. Transport mode leaves IP header visible. Exam test point.

5.5.h.3 · weight: high

Gateway-to-gateway IPsec always uses Tunnel mode. Entire original packet hidden inside the tunnel.

5.5.i.1 · weight: high

End-to-end IPsec (e.g., between two servers). Transport mode protects just the payload.

5.5.i.2 · weight: high

Generic Routing Encapsulation. Cisco tunneling protocol. Carries multicast/broadcast and multiple L3 protocols.

5.5.j.1 · weight: high

Pure IPsec is unicast only. Routing protocols (OSPF, EIGRP) use multicast, so pure IPsec can't run them.

5.5.j.2 · weight: high

Wrap traffic in GRE (adds multicast support) then encrypt with IPsec. Enables OSPF over VPN.

5.5.j.3 · weight: high

Dynamic Multipoint VPN. Cisco solution for scalable hub-and-spoke with dynamic spoke-to-spoke tunnels.

5.5.j.4 · weight: med

Matches source IP ONLY. Numbered 1-99, 1300-1999. Simple, broad filtering.

5.6.a.1 · weight: high

As close to DESTINATION as possible. Broad source matching means you'd block legitimate traffic if placed near source.

5.6.a.2 · weight: high

'access-list [1-99] {permit|deny} [source] [wildcard]'. Or named: 'ip access-list standard [name]'.

5.6.a.3 · weight: high

Shortcut for exact match. 'host 10.1.1.1' = '10.1.1.1 0.0.0.0'.

5.6.a.4 · weight: med

Shortcut for all. 'any' = '0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255'.

5.6.a.5 · weight: med

Matches source + destination + protocol + port. Numbered 100-199, 2000-2699. Granular filtering.

5.6.b.1 · weight: high

As close to SOURCE as possible. Granular match = can block specific traffic without affecting others.

5.6.b.2 · weight: high

'access-list [100-199] {permit|deny} [proto] [src wildcard] [dst wildcard] [operator port]'.

5.6.b.3 · weight: high

eq (equals), gt, lt, neq, range. Example: 'eq 80' matches HTTP. 'range 20 21' matches FTP.

5.6.b.4 · weight: high

Alternative to numbered. 'ip access-list {standard|extended} [name]' → sub-config mode.

5.6.c.1 · weight: high

Each ACE has a seq number (10, 20, 30...). Insert by specifying seq. Delete with 'no [seq#]'.

5.6.c.2 · weight: high

'ip access-list resequence [name] [start] [increment]'. Renumber entries for easier insertion.

5.6.c.3 · weight: med

INVERSE of subnet mask. 0 = must match, 1 = don't care. 255.255.255.0 → 0.0.0.255.

5.6.d.1 · weight: high

0.0.0.0 = exact IP match. 'host X.X.X.X' is shortcut for 'X.X.X.X 0.0.0.0'.

5.6.d.3 · weight: high

'host 10.1.1.1' = '10.1.1.1 0.0.0.0'. Exact single-host match.

5.6.e.1 · weight: high

'any' = '0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255'. Matches all IPs.

5.6.e.2 · weight: high

Exam uses both. 'permit host 10.1.1.1' and 'permit 10.1.1.1 0.0.0.0' are identical.

5.6.e.3 · weight: high

Every ACL has invisible 'deny any' at the end. Traffic NOT explicitly permitted = BLOCKED.

5.6.f.1 · weight: high

Optional but best practice — makes implicit deny visible. Also enables match-counter logging.

5.6.f.2 · weight: high

ACL only permits specific traffic → everything else silently dropped. Classic exam 'what's broken?' scenario.

5.6.f.3 · weight: high

Router evaluates ACEs in order (sequence number). First match wins. Stops processing immediately.

5.6.g.1 · weight: high

Specific rules must come BEFORE general rules. 'deny host X' above 'permit subnet' — reverse and host X would match 'permit' first.

5.6.g.2 · weight: high

Given packet + ACL, trace ACE-by-ACE until first match. Action at first match = the result.

5.6.g.3 · weight: high

Blocks all source traffic. Must place near destination so legitimate traffic isn't blocked en route.

5.6.h.1 · weight: high

Specific match (src+dst+proto+port). Place near source to save bandwidth by dropping early.

5.6.h.2 · weight: high

Wrong placement = traffic dropped unnecessarily OR consumes bandwidth before being dropped.

5.6.h.3 · weight: high

Given topology diagram, determine where to apply ACL. Standard → closest to dest. Extended → closest to source.

5.6.i.1 · weight: high

Read: standard or extended? Read: source + destination. Apply the placement rule. Check direction.

5.6.i.2 · weight: high

Traffic ENTERING the router through this interface. Before routing decision.

5.6.j.1 · weight: high

Traffic LEAVING the router through this interface. After routing decision.

5.6.j.2 · weight: high

Apply ACL on an interface. Must specify direction.

5.6.j.3 · weight: high

Direction is relative to the ROUTER, not the client. In = into router. Out = out of router.

5.6.j.4 · weight: high

Apply ACL to VTY lines: 'access-class [ACL] in'. Controls who can SSH/Telnet to the router.

5.6.k.1 · weight: high

Apply ACL to physical interface: 'ip access-group [ACL] {in|out}'. Filters transit traffic.

5.6.k.2 · weight: high

access-class for VTY, access-group for interfaces. Classic exam trap.

5.6.k.3 · weight: high

Under 'ip access-list' sub-config: 'no [seq#]'. Removes a single ACE without affecting others.

5.6.l.1 · weight: high

Type '[seq#] permit/deny ...' under the named ACL sub-config. Inserts at that sequence number.

5.6.l.2 · weight: high

Renumber existing ACEs to open space for inserts. E.g., 'resequence myacl 10 10' → 10, 20, 30...

5.6.l.3 · weight: med

Displays all ACLs and their entries with match counts. Primary ACL verification command.

5.6.m.1 · weight: high

Shows which ACL is applied on interface and direction. Useful for tracking application.

5.6.m.2 · weight: high

Like show access-lists but IPv4 only. Same output format.

5.6.m.3 · weight: med

L2 security feature. Blocks rogue DHCP servers. Only trusted ports forward DHCP offers.

5.7.a.1 · weight: high

Trusted = facing legit DHCP server. Untrusted = user ports, block DHCP server messages (offers).

5.7.a.2 · weight: high

IP ↔ MAC ↔ port ↔ VLAN. Built from observed DHCP transactions. Used by DAI and IP Source Guard.

5.7.a.3 · weight: high

Enables DHCP snooping globally on switch. Step 1.

5.7.b.1 · weight: high

Enables snooping per-VLAN. Both global AND per-VLAN required.

5.7.b.2 · weight: high

Interface command on trusted port (uplink toward DHCP server). Default = untrusted.

5.7.b.3 · weight: high

By default DHCP snooping inserts Option 82. Relay may reject it. Use 'no ip dhcp snooping information option' if needed.

5.7.b.4 · weight: med

DAI uses the DHCP snooping binding table to validate ARP replies. Without snooping, DAI has no data.

5.7.c.1 · weight: high

View the binding table: IP, MAC, port, VLAN, lease expiration.

5.7.c.2 · weight: high

Attacker requests many DHCP leases using spoofed MACs. Exhausts DHCP pool. Legit clients get denied.

5.7.d.1 · weight: high

DHCP snooping + port security (limit MACs) + rate limiting DHCP messages per port.

5.7.d.2 · weight: high

Per-port rate limit for DHCP packets. Prevents starvation and rogue flooding.

5.7.d.3 · weight: med

L2 security. Validates ARP replies against DHCP snooping binding table. Stops ARP spoofing.

5.7.e.1 · weight: high

DAI uses the binding table that DHCP snooping builds. Enable snooping FIRST.

5.7.e.2 · weight: high

Enable DAI per VLAN.

5.7.e.3 · weight: high

Interface command. Trusted ports (uplinks) bypass ARP inspection. User ports = untrusted (default).

5.7.e.4 · weight: high

DHCP snooping → populates binding table → DAI reads binding table → validates ARP.

5.7.f.1 · weight: high

Port connecting to legit DHCP server + ARP source = trusted for both DHCP snooping AND DAI.

5.7.f.2 · weight: high

Port security + DHCP snooping + DAI + IP Source Guard = comprehensive access-layer security.

5.7.f.3 · weight: med

L2 feature. Limits which MAC addresses can connect on a port. Prevents rogue device attachment.

5.7.g.1 · weight: high

Default 1 when enabled. Configurable with 'switchport port-security maximum [N]'.

5.7.g.2 · weight: high

Stops MAC flooding attacks + enforces 'one-device-per-port' policies. First line of access-layer defense.

5.7.g.3 · weight: high

Port must be ACCESS mode. 'switchport mode access' first. Fails on trunk or dynamic.

5.7.h.1 · weight: high

Interface command. Enables port security on the port.

5.7.h.2 · weight: high

'port-security maximum [N]' sets max MACs. 'port-security mac-address [MAC]' statically allows specific MAC.

5.7.h.3 · weight: high

(1) mode access, (2) port-security enable, (3) max, (4) mac-address or sticky, (5) violation mode.

5.7.h.4 · weight: high

Auto-learn connected MACs and ADD them to running-config. Convenient — no manual MAC entry.

5.7.i.1 · weight: high

Sticky MACs are in RUNNING-config. Must 'copy run start' or they're lost on reboot (re-learned after).

5.7.i.2 · weight: high

Static = manually typed in config. Sticky = auto-learned but saved as static. Both persistent.

5.7.i.3 · weight: high

Drops violating frames silently. NO log, NO SNMP trap. Port stays up. Weakest enforcement.

5.7.j.1 · weight: high

Drops violating frames + LOGS syslog/SNMP trap + increments counter. Port stays up.

5.7.j.2 · weight: high

Port → err-disabled. Requires manual intervention. Logs + increments violation count. DEFAULT.

5.7.j.3 · weight: high

Interface command. Takes 'protect', 'restrict', or 'shutdown'. Default is 'shutdown'.

5.7.j.4 · weight: high

'shutdown' then 'no shutdown' on the port. Returns to normal operation.

5.7.k.1 · weight: high

Global command to auto-recover. E.g., 'errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation' enables auto-recovery from port security.

5.7.k.2 · weight: high

Time (seconds) before auto-recovery attempt. Default 300s.

5.7.k.3 · weight: med

Proves WHO you are. Credentials verified (password, certificate, token).

5.8.a.1 · weight: high

Defines WHAT you can do. Privilege levels, command restrictions, access scope.

5.8.a.2 · weight: high

Records WHAT you DID. Audit trail of actions for compliance and forensics.

5.8.a.3 · weight: high

Authenticate first → Authorize → Account. Order matters — can't authorize unknown user.

5.8.a.4 · weight: med

0-15. 15 = full access (enable). 1 = user-mode. Custom levels let you authorize specific commands.

5.8.b.1 · weight: high

TACACS+ feature. Authorize/deny individual IOS commands per user. RADIUS can't do this.

5.8.b.2 · weight: high

Assign commands to custom privilege levels. Enables role-based access without full enable rights.

5.8.b.3 · weight: med

Audit trail. Records who logged in, what commands they ran, when they logged out. For compliance and forensics.

5.8.c.1 · weight: high

Commands, exec (session start/stop), system events. Each can be logged separately.

5.8.c.2 · weight: med

HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOX require audit trails. Accounting provides the evidence for audits.

5.8.c.3 · weight: med

Cisco-proprietary. Full packet encryption. Separates AAA. Per-command authorization. Ideal for router/switch mgmt.

5.8.d.1 · weight: high

Open standard. 802.1X wired/wireless, VPN authentication, WPA2-Enterprise. Multi-vendor.

5.8.d.2 · weight: high

UDP 1812/1813. Open standard. Only password encrypted. Combines auth+authz. Network ACCESS.

5.8.e.1 · weight: high

TCP 49. Cisco proprietary. FULL packet encryption. Separates AAA. Device ADMIN.

5.8.e.2 · weight: high

RADIUS=UDP 1812/1813. TACACS+=TCP 49. This is tested every exam cycle.

5.8.e.3 · weight: high

Client device requesting access. Runs 802.1X software. Provides credentials.

5.8.f.1 · weight: high

Switch/WLC. Intermediate device. Forwards credentials from supplicant to authentication server. Enforces decision.

5.8.f.2 · weight: high

Typically RADIUS server (Cisco ISE, Microsoft NPS). Validates credentials against user database.

5.8.f.3 · weight: high

Extensible Authentication Protocol. Framework 802.1X uses. EAP-TLS, PEAP, EAP-FAST are common methods.

5.8.f.4 · weight: med

Original wireless encryption. RC4 cipher. COMPLETELY BROKEN. Never use.

5.9.a.1 · weight: high

Initialization Vector is only 24 bits. Reuses within ~5000 frames. Attacker captures enough → cracks key in minutes.

5.9.a.2 · weight: high

Same key across all devices. Never rotated. If one device compromised, entire network is exposed.

5.9.a.3 · weight: med

Wi-Fi Protected Access v1. Uses TKIP (still RC4-based). Temporary fix between WEP and WPA2. Now deprecated.

5.9.b.1 · weight: high

Made WEP-era hardware usable. Added per-packet keys and MIC. But TKIP itself has known weaknesses now.

5.9.b.2 · weight: med

WEP (RC4) → WPA (TKIP/RC4) → WPA2 (AES/CCMP) → WPA3 (AES/GCMP + SAE).

5.9.b.3 · weight: high

IEEE 802.11i standard. Current production baseline. Uses AES-CCMP encryption.

5.9.c.1 · weight: high

WPA2's strong cipher. Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol. Replaces TKIP.

5.9.c.2 · weight: high

Any enterprise deploying today should at minimum use WPA2 + AES. TKIP only for legacy compat.

5.9.c.3 · weight: high

Newest WPA standard. Adds SAE, OWE, forward secrecy. Drops WPS. Currently rolling out.

5.9.d.1 · weight: high

Simultaneous Authentication of Equals. Replaces PSK handshake. Forward secrecy; resists offline dictionary attacks.

5.9.d.2 · weight: high

Opportunistic Wireless Encryption. Encrypts open networks (no password). Replaces plaintext on public WiFi.

5.9.d.3 · weight: high

Higher-security mode. 192-bit crypto for government/financial. Uses CNSA suite.

5.9.d.4 · weight: med

PSK (pre-shared key). Everyone uses the same password. Simple. Used in home/SOHO.

5.9.e.1 · weight: high

802.1X. Each user has unique credentials. Requires RADIUS. Used in corporate networks.

5.9.e.2 · weight: high

'Home network' → Personal. 'Corporate with AD' → Enterprise. 'Small office no RADIUS' → Personal.

5.9.e.3 · weight: high

Client device (laptop, phone). Has 802.1X software configured with credentials or cert.

5.9.f.1 · weight: high

The WLC or AP. Forwards EAP messages between supplicant and RADIUS server.

5.9.f.2 · weight: high

RADIUS server (ISE/NPS). Validates credentials against AD or internal database. Returns Accept/Reject.

5.9.f.3 · weight: high

Primary WLC management interface. Browser-based. Access via management IP on HTTPS.

5.10.a.1 · weight: high

First-time WLC config via console. After IP assigned, switch to HTTPS GUI for full management.

5.10.a.2 · weight: med

Some WLC CLI exists but most daily ops are GUI-driven. Different philosophy from IOS.

5.10.a.3 · weight: med

Profile Name (internal), SSID (broadcast), WLAN ID (1-512). All three required.

5.10.b.1 · weight: high

Profile is internal label; SSID is what users see. Can match or differ.

5.10.b.2 · weight: high

Default after creation is DISABLED. Set Status = Enabled to start broadcasting.

5.10.b.3 · weight: high

Correct answer for WPA2-Personal scenarios. Layer 2 Security = WPA+WPA2, Encryption = AES, Auth = PSK.

5.10.c.1 · weight: high

If WLC shows TKIP enabled, that's the security issue. Disable TKIP, enable only AES.

5.10.c.2 · weight: high

Security tab → Layer 2 Security → WPA+WPA2 → check AES → Auth Key Mgmt = PSK → enter key.

5.10.c.3 · weight: high

Each WLAN maps to a dynamic interface, which is tied to a VLAN. Clients get IPs from that VLAN's DHCP.

5.10.d.1 · weight: high

WLAN's General tab has 'Interface/Interface Group' dropdown. Select the dynamic interface.

5.10.d.2 · weight: high

Trunk between switch and WLC must allow the VLANs mapped to WLANs. Common failure scenario.

5.10.d.3 · weight: high

WLAN exists in config but SSID is NOT broadcast. APs don't announce it in beacons.

5.10.e.1 · weight: high

WLANs tab → select WLAN → General → Status = Enabled → Apply. Now SSID appears.

5.10.e.2 · weight: high

Lab sim scenario: 'new SSID invisible'. First check: WLAN status. Cheapest easy-point answer.

5.10.e.3 · weight: high

40-80% of outages from human error. Automation applies tested templates → fewer typos, misconfigs.

6.1.a.1 · weight: high

Speed, consistency, scalability, self-healing, reduced errors. Test emphasizes these.

6.1.a.2 · weight: high

Deploy 100 switches in minutes instead of days. Push config changes across fleet in seconds.

6.1.b.1 · weight: high

Plug in new device → it auto-fetches its config from the network. No manual staging.

6.1.b.2 · weight: high

Same effort to manage 1,000 devices as 10. One template applies to entire fleet.

6.1.c.1 · weight: high

Manual ops scale linearly with devices. Automation scales sub-linearly — more devices, minimal extra effort.

6.1.c.2 · weight: med

Same template → identical configs across devices. Eliminates 'snowflake' devices with unique tweaks.

6.1.d.1 · weight: high

Devices gradually differ from intended config. Automation detects drift and re-aligns to template.

6.1.d.2 · weight: high

Automation tools log exactly what config each device has at any time. Easy to prove compliance.

6.1.d.3 · weight: med

Event-driven automation. Detects issues (interface down, congestion) and auto-remediates without human.

6.1.e.1 · weight: high

Mean Time To Recovery. Automation cuts MTTR from hours to minutes/seconds.

6.1.e.2 · weight: med

Link down → automation reroutes + opens ticket. High CPU → script kills runaway process.

6.1.e.3 · weight: med

Distributed control plane. Each device independently runs routing/STP. CLI per-device config. Manual.

6.2.a.1 · weight: high

Centralized control plane. Controller pushes config to devices. API-driven. Policy-based.

6.2.a.2 · weight: high

Traditional: per-device CLI, distributed. SDN: centralized controller, APIs, single pane of glass.

6.2.a.3 · weight: high

Centralized brain. Hosts control plane logic. Pushes forwarding info to devices via southbound APIs.

6.2.b.1 · weight: high

Switches/routers in SDN only forward traffic. No local control decisions.

6.2.b.2 · weight: high

Controller → devices. Examples: OpenFlow, NETCONF, RESTCONF. Push config and forwarding rules.

6.2.b.3 · weight: high

One dashboard manages all devices. Replaces SSH-ing to each device individually. SDN buzzword.

6.2.c.1 · weight: high

Describe DESIRED state (intent) → controller translates to device config. Declarative, not procedural.

6.2.c.2 · weight: high

Cisco's IBN platform. Network-wide controller (not just wireless). On-prem, manages wired + wireless.

6.2.d.1 · weight: high

Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance. Memorize the acronym.

6.2.d.2 · weight: high

DNA Center exposes REST APIs for automation. Integrates with external systems via HTTPS/JSON.

6.2.d.3 · weight: med

Control = routing decisions (who to send where). Data = actual packet forwarding. SDN separates them.

6.3.a.1 · weight: high

Traditional: each device has both. SDN: control centralized on controller, data stays distributed on devices.

6.3.a.2 · weight: high

Third plane. Config, monitoring. SSH, SNMP, NETCONF live here.

6.3.a.3 · weight: med

Controller ↔ applications. REST APIs typically. Apps use these to program the network.

6.3.b.1 · weight: high

Controller ↔ devices. OpenFlow, NETCONF, RESTCONF. Push device config and rules.

6.3.b.2 · weight: high

Apps are 'up top' (north), devices are 'below' (south). Controller in the middle.

6.3.b.3 · weight: high

Physical network. Actual devices, links, routing protocols. Foundation.

6.3.c.1 · weight: high

Virtual network built on top of underlay. VXLAN tunnels. Logically independent from physical paths.

6.3.c.2 · weight: high

Underlay + overlay together. Integrated programmable network. SD-Access, ACI, etc.

6.3.c.3 · weight: high

Virtual Extensible LAN. L2 frames tunneled over L3 networks. Primary DC overlay technology.

6.3.d.1 · weight: high

24-bit identifier. 16 million segments — vs VLAN's 12-bit / 4094. Massive scale.

6.3.d.2 · weight: high

VXLAN encapsulates in UDP 4789. Travels over any L3 infrastructure.

6.3.d.3 · weight: high

Cisco campus fabric. Underlay = IS-IS. Overlay = VXLAN. Control plane = LISP. Policy = CTS/SGTs.

6.3.e.1 · weight: high

Locator/ID Separation Protocol. Tracks endpoint-to-location mapping. Enables mobility without readdressing.

6.3.e.2 · weight: med

Cisco TrustSec with Scalable Group Tags. Policy based on user/device group, not IP. Microsegmentation.

6.3.e.3 · weight: med

AI that CREATES output — configs, summaries, troubleshooting suggestions. ChatGPT-style assistance for netops.

6.4.a.1 · weight: high

Config generation from natural language, log summarization, incident triage, documentation.

6.4.a.2 · weight: med

AI that FORECASTS — detects anomalies, predicts failures, trends traffic. Proactive ops.

6.4.b.1 · weight: high

Generative CREATES (content). Predictive FORECASTS (outcomes/anomalies). Different use cases.

6.4.b.2 · weight: high

ML detects patterns that deviate from baseline. Catches zero-day attacks, hardware degradation.

6.4.b.3 · weight: med

Learns normal patterns over time (daily/weekly/monthly). Adapts. Unlike static 'CPU > 80%' thresholds.

6.4.c.1 · weight: high

ML-driven monitoring. Detects anomalies, suggests root causes, flags degradation before failure.

6.4.c.2 · weight: high

AI for IT Operations. Event correlation across many sources → automated root-cause analysis → remediation.

6.4.d.1 · weight: high

Connects seemingly unrelated events to find true cause. Dozens of alarms → one root problem.

6.4.d.2 · weight: high

AIOps doesn't just alert — it tells you WHY. 'Link X down caused 47 downstream alarms'.

6.4.d.3 · weight: med

REpresentational State Transfer. Architecture style for APIs. Uses HTTP methods + URIs + JSON/XML.

6.5.a.1 · weight: high

Each request contains all info needed. Server doesn't store client session between requests.

6.5.a.2 · weight: high

Separation of concerns. Client handles UI; server handles data and logic. Independent evolution.

6.5.a.3 · weight: high

Each resource has a unique URI. /api/v1/devices/42 represents device ID 42. Nouns, not verbs.

6.5.a.4 · weight: high

Add a new resource. 'POST /devices' creates a device. Server assigns ID.

6.5.b.1 · weight: high

Retrieve resource(s). 'GET /devices' lists all. 'GET /devices/42' gets one. Read-only, no side effects.

6.5.b.2 · weight: high

Replace an existing resource entirely. 'PUT /devices/42' overwrites device 42.

6.5.b.3 · weight: high

Modify specific fields. 'PATCH /devices/42' updates only the specified attributes.

6.5.b.4 · weight: high

Remove resource. 'DELETE /devices/42' destroys the device entry.

6.5.b.5 · weight: high

Success. GET/PUT/DELETE returned what was expected.

6.5.c.1 · weight: high

POST succeeded. New resource was created. Response often includes location header with new URI.

6.5.c.2 · weight: high

Client sent malformed request. Syntax or validation error.

6.5.c.3 · weight: high

Missing or invalid credentials. Authentication required.

6.5.c.4 · weight: high

Authenticated but NOT authorized. Valid creds but not allowed to access this resource.

6.5.c.5 · weight: high

Resource doesn't exist at that URI.

6.5.c.6 · weight: high

Server-side failure. Not client's fault — server bug or crash.

6.5.c.7 · weight: high

4xx = CLIENT error (you sent bad request). 5xx = SERVER error (server failed to handle valid request).

6.5.c.8 · weight: high

Static secret string sent in header. Simplest auth. Fine for internal or low-sensitivity APIs.

6.5.d.1 · weight: high

Token obtained after login, sent in 'Authorization: Bearer [token]' header. Session-based.

6.5.d.2 · weight: high

Delegated access. User grants limited permissions to third-party apps without sharing password.

6.5.d.3 · weight: high

Base64-encoded username:password in header. Not encryption — must be combined with HTTPS. Legacy.

6.5.d.4 · weight: med

JavaScript Object Notation. Most common REST data format. { } for objects, [ ] for arrays.

6.5.e.1 · weight: high

Extensible Markup Language. Used by NETCONF. Tag-based: value. More verbose than JSON.

6.5.e.2 · weight: high

YAML Ain't Markup Language. Human-friendly. Used by Ansible playbooks. Indentation-based.

6.5.e.3 · weight: high

{ } JSON. < /> XML. --- + indented lines = YAML. Exam may show a snippet and ask format.

6.5.e.4 · weight: high

HTTP verb + URI + headers + body. Example: 'POST /api/devices' + JSON body = create a device.

6.5.f.1 · weight: high

Given 'PUT /devices/42' → Update. Given 'Create a device' → POST /devices. Practice both directions.

6.5.f.2 · weight: high

Config management tool. AGENTLESS (uses SSH). YAML playbooks. PUSH model. Idempotent.

6.6.a.1 · weight: high

No software installed on managed devices. Uses SSH/WinRM. Network-friendly.

6.6.a.2 · weight: high

Running a playbook multiple times produces the same result. Safe to re-run.

6.6.a.3 · weight: high

Ansible = agentless, push, YAML. Puppet/Chef = agent-based, pull, Ruby DSL.

6.6.a.4 · weight: high

List of managed devices. Can be static file or dynamic (cloud API). Groups devices by role/env.

6.6.b.1 · weight: high

YAML file defining a sequence of tasks to run against devices. The 'what to do'.

6.6.b.2 · weight: high

Individual action (install package, copy file, run command). Playbooks call modules.

6.6.b.3 · weight: high

Reusable bundle of tasks/files/templates. 'role: webserver' applies all web-related config.

6.6.b.4 · weight: med

Infrastructure as Code tool. CREATES/DESTROYS cloud resources. HCL language. Declarative. Maintains state file.

6.6.c.1 · weight: high

Define infrastructure in code files. Version-controlled, repeatable, reviewable. Terraform is the canonical IaC tool.

6.6.c.2 · weight: high

Tracks what resources Terraform manages. Required to plan and apply changes correctly.

6.6.c.3 · weight: med

Declarative (Terraform): describe end state. Procedural (Ansible): describe steps to reach state.

6.6.c.4 · weight: high

Initialize working directory. Downloads provider plugins. First command you run.

6.6.d.1 · weight: high

Preview changes. Shows what Terraform WOULD do. Safety net — always review before apply.

6.6.d.2 · weight: high

Execute the plan. Creates/modifies/destroys resources to match config.

6.6.d.3 · weight: high

Tear down all managed resources. Useful for test environments. Irreversible.

6.6.d.4 · weight: high

Create VMs, VPCs, cloud infra → Terraform. Configure existing routers/servers → Ansible.

6.6.e.1 · weight: high

Real workflow: Terraform creates VMs → Ansible configures them. Each tool's strength.

6.6.e.2 · weight: high

Server pushes config to managed devices on demand. Ansible uses push. Immediate application.

6.6.f.1 · weight: high

Agent on device periodically pulls config from master. Puppet/Chef use pull. Continuous reconciliation.

6.6.f.2 · weight: high

Agent = software running on device. Agentless = remote connection (SSH/WinRM). Ansible is agentless.

6.6.f.3 · weight: high

Key-value pairs. Keys in quotes. Values: string, number, boolean, null, object, array.

6.7.a.1 · weight: high

{ } braces. Key-value pairs inside. Example: {"name": "R1", "type": "router"}.

6.7.a.2 · weight: high

[ ] brackets. Ordered list of values. Example: ["R1", "R2", "R3"].

6.7.a.3 · weight: high

Curly braces. Collection of unordered key-value pairs. Access by key.

6.7.b.1 · weight: high

Square brackets. Ordered list. Access by numeric index (0-based).

6.7.b.2 · weight: high

{ → object. [ → array. First character of snippet tells you the type.

6.7.b.3 · weight: high

string, number, boolean, null, object, array. Memorize all six.

6.7.c.1 · weight: high

'true' / 'false' only. 'True' or 'FALSE' = INVALID JSON. Must be lowercase.

6.7.c.2 · weight: high

JSON has no 'date' type (use string). No 'undefined' (use null). Common exam traps.

6.7.c.3 · weight: high

Objects inside objects, arrays inside objects, etc. Exam shows nested structure and asks for a value.

6.7.d.1 · weight: high

Dot notation + brackets. data.devices[0].name = first device's name field.

6.7.d.2 · weight: high

Read outer keys first → drill down. Object key → array index [N] → nested key → value.

6.7.d.3 · weight: high

Strings + keys MUST use double quotes " ". Single quotes ' ' are INVALID JSON.

6.7.e.1 · weight: high

[1, 2, 3,] is INVALID. Final element cannot have trailing comma. JavaScript allows it; JSON does NOT.

6.7.e.2 · weight: high

JSON does NOT support // or /* */ comments. Any comment = invalid JSON.

6.7.e.3 · weight: high

'text' INVALID. True/FALSE INVALID. Must be: "text", true, false.

6.7.e.4 · weight: high

Scan for: double quotes only, no trailing commas, no comments, lowercase booleans. Any violation = invalid.
